def_en: a metal fastening that confines the wrists or ankles; something that restricts freedom
def_zh: iron shackles, shackle of tradition, break the shackles
quote_en: The prisoner's shackles clanked against the floor as he shuffled toward the judge.
quote_zh: "Chained head to toe, head to toe, in exponential misery." (Ajarry)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "shackle" both literally and metaphorically: the iron on Cora's wrists is inseparable from the psychological shackles of fear and inherited trauma that bind generations.
ex_zh: By opening the novel with Ajarry in shackles and ending with Cora's uncertain freedom, Whitehead frames the entire narrative as a question: can a body ever be truly free when the mind still wears the shackles of the past?

def_en: to obstruct or hinder the progress of something
def_zh: stymie progress, stymie an attempt, stymie a plan
quote_en: The detective's investigation was stymied by a complete lack of physical evidence.
quote_zh: "Ajarry twice tried to kill herself on the voyage to America, once by denying herself food and then again by drowning. The sailors stymied her both times, versed in the schemes and inclination of chattel." (Ajarry)
ex_en: Whitehead shows that every attempt at self-liberation—even suicide—is systematically stymied by the machinery of slavery, turning the body into a prison from which there is no escape.
ex_zh: The sailors' ability to stymie Ajarry's suicide attempts reveals the slave system's total control over the enslaved body, a control so complete that even death is not a choice but a privilege to be denied.

def_en: an item of personal property; historically, a slave considered as property
def_zh: chattel slavery, human chattel, as mere chattel
quote_en: In the eyes of the law at that time, enslaved people were treated as chattel rather than human beings.
quote_zh: "The sailors stymied her both times, versed in the schemes and inclination of chattel." (Ajarry)
ex_en: By persistently using "chattel" to describe enslaved people, Whitehead exposes the dehumanizing logic of the plantation economy, where human worth is reduced to a price on a bill of sale.
ex_zh: The novel's use of legal and economic vocabulary—"chattel," "liquidate," "property"—creates an ironic contrast with the Declaration of Independence's claim that "all men are created equal," forcing readers to confront the gap between American ideals and practice.

def_en: the state of being enslaved or physically restrained
def_zh: in bondage, escape from bondage, spiritual bondage
quote_en: The novel explores the psychological bondage that persists long after physical chains are removed.
quote_zh: "In her stories, Isay and Sidoo and the rest somehow bought their way out of bondage and lived as free men and women in the City of Pennsylvania." (Ajarry)
ex_en: Whitehead distinguishes between physical bondage and the internalized submission that keeps the enslaved compliant even when no overseer is watching—the true horror of the system.
ex_zh: The underground railroad represents a literal escape from bondage, but the novel repeatedly asks whether any character, even those who reach the North, ever fully escapes the psychic bondage of having been owned.

def_en: a person who stares openly in a foolish or rude manner
def_zh: idle gawkers, a crowd of gawkers, gawkers and bystanders
quote_en: The accident drew a crowd of gawkers who did nothing to help.
quote_zh: "Onlookers chewed fresh oysters and hot corn as the auctioneers shouted into the air. The slaves stood naked on the platform." (Ajarry)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "gawker" to indict the ordinary white citizens whose passive complicity—watching, consuming, doing nothing—enables the slave system to function.
ex_zh: The gawkers at the auction foreshadow the Friday night crowds in North Carolina who watch lynchings as entertainment, revealing that spectatorship is itself a form of violence.

def_en: a chain used to restrain a prisoner; to confine or restrict
def_zh: fetter with chains, break the fetters, fetters of convention
quote_en: The outdated traditions fettered the company's ability to innovate.
quote_zh: "She was branded, not for the first or last time, and fettered to the rest of the day's acquisitions." (Ajarry)
ex_en: Whitehead transforms the literal fetter into a metaphor for the limitations that race, gender, and class impose on human possibility in nineteenth-century America.
ex_zh: The novel's recurring image of fetters—on Ajarry, on Cora, on the captives in the slave ship exhibit—creates a visual leitmotif that ties individual suffering to the larger machinery of American bondage.

def_en: to convert assets into cash; to eliminate or kill
def_zh: liquidate assets, liquidate a debt, liquidate opponents
quote_en: The company was forced to liquidate its holdings after declaring bankruptcy.
quote_zh: "Her first master got swindled... in the end Ajarry was another asset liquidated by order of the magistrate." (Ajarry)
ex_en: Whitehead's use of the cold commercial verb "liquidate" to describe the sale of human beings reveals the economic rationalism that underlies chattel slavery: people are assets to be cashed out.
ex_zh: The novel forces readers to recognize that the language of modern finance—liquidation, assets, property—was perfected on the bodies of the enslaved, making American capitalism inseparable from American slavery.

def_en: a large estate farmed by resident laborers, historically enslaved people
def_zh: cotton plantation, plantation owner, plantation economy
quote_en: The family had owned the plantation for five generations before the war.
quote_zh: "Finally, Georgia. A representative of the Randall plantation bought her for two hundred and ninety-two dollars." (Ajarry)
ex_en: Whitehead reimagines the plantation not as the pastoral setting of lost-cause mythology but as a total institution designed to extract maximum labor while systematically destroying human identity.
ex_zh: Each section of the novel presents a different model of racial control, but the plantation remains the ur-form—the original machine whose logic of extraction and dehumanization spreads to every state Cora visits.

def_en: easily bent or influenced; flexible
def_zh: pliable material, pliable personality, pliable and obedient
quote_en: The young politician proved pliable under pressure from party leaders.
quote_zh: "The trader called upon the tobacco plantation looking for slaves of breeding age, preferably with all their teeth and of pliable disposition." (Ajarry)
ex_en: Slave traders prized pliable disposition above all else, for a compliant slave required less breaking and yielded more years of labor before death or despair took hold.
ex_zh: The novel suggests that Cora survives not because she is pliable but because she is stubbornly resistant—her refusal to be shaped by the plantation is precisely what makes her a fugitive.

def_en: to be in agreement; to make mocking remarks
def_zh: jibe with the facts, jibe at someone's expense, make a jibe
quote_en: His account of the incident did not jibe with what the other witnesses reported.
quote_zh: "The image of her grandmother assaulting another slave didn't jibe with Cora's recollections of the woman." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead's narrative exposes the fundamental contradiction between the ideology of paternalistic slavery and its brutal reality, showing how the two cannot jibe.
ex_zh: The novel repeatedly stages moments where official stories—the happy plantation, the civilizing mission—fail to jibe with lived experience, creating a gap that fugitives like Cora slip through.

def_en: extremely bad, severe, or degrading; showing utter hopelessness
def_zh: abject poverty, abject misery, abject apology
quote_en: The refugees lived in abject poverty, without clean water or shelter.
quote_zh: "Off to Hob with those who had been crippled by the overseers' punishments... off to Hob with strays." (Georgia)
ex_en: The Hob women inhabited a state of abject misery that the plantation had engineered—too broken for the fields, too wretched to be anything but a warning.
ex_zh: The Hob women function as a cautionary exhibit within the novel, showing what happens when the system succeeds in making a person abject—yet Cora's survival proves that even abjection can be a form of camouflage.

def_en: to behave or conduct oneself in a particular manner
def_zh: comport oneself with dignity, comport with standards, well-comported
quote_en: The professor comported himself with dignity even under hostile questioning.
quote_zh: "Old Abraham, who was not old at all but who had comported himself in the manner of an elderly misanthrope since he first learned to sit up." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead shows that how a character comports themselves on the plantation is a survival strategy, not a reflection of innate character—the mask is not the face.
ex_zh: The contrast between how Cora comports herself in Georgia (silent, watchful) and in South Carolina (learning, adapting) reveals that comportment is situational, a performance demanded by power rather than an expression of self.

def_en: a person who dislikes or distrusts humankind
def_zh: bitter misanthrope, cynical misanthrope, misanthrope recluse
quote_en: The old man lived as a recluse, his reputation as a misanthrope well earned.
quote_zh: "Old Abraham, who was not old at all but who had comported himself in the manner of an elderly misanthrope since he first learned to sit up." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead complicates the figure of the misanthrope by making Old Abraham a man whose cynicism is a shield against the hope that had only ever brought him pain.
ex_zh: The novel's misanthropes—Old Abraham, Ridgeway—reveal that hatred of humanity can be either a defense against trauma or a weapon of oppression, and the distinction lies in who the hatred serves.

def_en: feeling or revealing crude sexual desire
def_zh: lascivious glance, lascivious behavior, lascivious intent
quote_en: The manager's lascivious comments created a hostile work environment.
quote_zh: "Young women whispered how they watched her slink away from the cabins on the full moon, to the woods, where she fornicated with donkeys and goats." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "lascivious" to show how sexual predation is not a deviation from slavery but one of its core features—the master's right to the bodies of the enslaved.
ex_zh: The novel refuses to sensationalize sexual violence, but the repeated presence of lascivious white men (Terrance, the slave traders) establishes that the female slave's body is never safe, even in supposed refuges.

def_en: to make a liquid turbid or muddy; to disturb or agitate
def_zh: roil the waters, roil emotions, roil the public
quote_en: Political scandals continued to roil the nation throughout the summer.
quote_zh: "The dirt at her feet had a story, the oldest story Cora knew." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses the visceral verb "roil" to describe the effect of fugitives on the social order—they disturb the settled sediment of racial hierarchy, making clear what was previously hidden.
ex_zh: The novel's structure, which moves Cora through different states with different systems of racial control, shows how each new environment is roiled by her very presence, as if a single fugitive carries enough disruption to unsettle an entire society.

def_en: the responsible management of something entrusted to one's care
def_zh: environmental stewardship, financial stewardship, stewardship of resources
quote_en: The tribe has maintained stewardship of these lands for over a thousand years.
quote_zh: "The Randall brothers had flipped a coin years ago to determine stewardship of each half of the plantation." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead ironizes the word "stewardship" by applying it to slaveholders—they claim to be responsible stewards of their human property, but the novel shows this as self-serving hypocrisy.
ex_zh: The contrast between James's calculated stewardship and Terrance's chaotic cruelty reveals that there is no good stewardship of slavery, only degrees of evil; the very concept is a contradiction in terms.

def_en: nervous or easily frightened; fickle and unpredictable
def_zh: skittish horse, skittish investor, skittish behavior
quote_en: Investors remained skittish after the market's sudden collapse.
quote_zh: "The children peeked between their elders, making bets they had nothing to back up with... The children peeked between their elders, making bets they had nothing to back up with." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "skittish" to describe the hypervigilance of the enslaved, a constant state of alertness that is itself a form of psychological torture.
ex_zh: The novel suggests that skittishness is not a character flaw but a rational response to an environment where danger is unpredictable and can come from any direction at any time—the slaves are not paranoid; the system really is out to get them.

def_en: a sudden and unaccountable change of mood or behavior
def_zh: mere caprice, caprice of fortune, caprice of a tyrant
quote_en: The dictator ruled by caprice, executing loyal servants one day and promoting traitors the next.
quote_zh: "No one minded his caprices. It was enough that he was the oldest colored man they had ever met." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead shows that the master's caprice is not a departure from the logic of slavery but its essence: the absolute power to act without reason or accountability.
ex_zh: The novel repeatedly contrasts the caprice of individual masters with the systematic rationality of the plantation economy, revealing a fundamental instability: the system depends on arbitrary violence, but arbitrary violence also threatens to destroy the system's productivity.

def_en: a state of dreamy meditation or fanciful thinking
def_zh: lost in reverie, brief reverie, romantic reverie
quote_en: She was lost in reverie, imagining a life far from the factory walls.
quote_zh: "Sometimes a slave will be lost in a brief eddy of liberation. In the sway of a sudden reverie among the furrows." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead treats reverie as both a necessary psychological escape and a dangerous distraction—moments of imagined freedom make bondage bearable but also make its return more crushing.
ex_zh: The novel's structure, which moves from Georgia's plantation to South Carolina's false utopia to North Carolina's terror, suggests that every reverie of freedom in the South is a trap waiting to spring.

def_en: a person's face or facial expression; to tolerate or approve
def_zh: stern countenance, friendly countenance, countenance bad behavior
quote_en: The judge refused to countenance any further delays in the proceedings.
quote_zh: "James favored the mother, stout as a barrel and just as firm in countenance." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "countenance" as both noun and verb to explore the theme of silent complicity: the face that shows nothing is still countenancing evil.
ex_zh: The novel suggests that to countenance (tolerate) slavery is to have a countenance (face) that reveals nothing—the blank expression of the bystander who does nothing is itself a moral choice.

def_en: an unprincipled or wicked person; morally depraved
def_zh: old reprobate, reprobate behavior, reprobate and scoundrel
quote_en: The old reprobate had outlived all his friends and most of his enemies.
quote_zh: "The master and his unlikely pupil started with simple rhymes... as his tutor had been a reprobate who had been chased from every decent position he had ever held." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead complicates the figure of the reprobate by suggesting that moral depravity can sometimes produce unintended good—the corrupt tutor becomes an accidental liberator.
ex_zh: The novel refuses simple moral categories: a reprobate can be the vehicle for enlightenment, while upstanding citizens participate in lynchings, forcing readers to question what "reprobate" really means in a slave society.

def_en: to make confused or unable to think clearly
def_zh: addle the mind, addle one's wits, addle-brained
quote_en: Years of isolation had addled his mind to the point where he could no longer hold a conversation.
quote_zh: "Michael's ability never amounted to more than a parlor trick... Some torture or punishment had addled his senses." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "addle" to describe a specific form of psychological damage caused by slavery: not just suffering but the erosion of the ability to perceive reality accurately.
ex_zh: The novel suggests that the most insidious effect of slavery is that it addles the distinction between safety and danger, friend and enemy, hope and delusion—making the enslaved complicit in their own confinement.

def_en: to conceal one's true motives or feelings; to pretend
def_zh: dissemble one's intentions, dissemble ignorance, skill at dissembling
quote_en: The politician dissembled skillfully, never revealing his true position on the issue.
quote_zh: "Crooked Ava had not lost her power to dissemble since her days of harassing Cora—she hooted and stomped as if it were the height of the Christmas celebrations." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead shows that dissembling is not a moral failing in the enslaved but a survival skill: the ability to hide one's true thoughts is the difference between living and dying.
ex_zh: The novel's many scenes of performance—slaves dancing for the masters, Cora pretending to be Bessie, the coon shows—all revolve around the art of dissembling, revealing that the entire social order is built on a shared fiction.

def_en: to skip or dance about in a lively manner; a playful leap
def_zh: caper about, caper with joy, criminal caper
quote_en: The children capered across the lawn, chasing fireflies into the dusk.
quote_zh: "Oh, how they capered and hollered, shouted and hopped! Certainly this was the most lively song they had ever heard." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "caper" to expose the grotesque theatricality of plantation life, where every display of happiness is a coerced performance for the master's amusement.
ex_zh: The novel forces readers to see the capering slave as a tragic figure: the more joyfully he dances, the more complete his submission, turning the very expression of life into an advertisement for death.

def_en: to smile in a silly, self-conscious, or affected way
def_zh: simper at, simpering smile, simper with embarrassment
quote_en: The servant simpered at every word her master spoke, desperate to please.
quote_zh: "Chester simpered and bowed down before the white man. 'Sorry, master! Sorry, master!'" (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "simper" to capture the pathetic futility of appeasement—the simpering slave hopes that submission will deflect violence, but it never does.
ex_zh: The novel's treatment of Chester's simper and the resulting beating demonstrates that for the slave, no amount of deference is enough; the master's violence is not a response to behavior but an assertion of power that seeks any excuse.

def_en: occurring at an inconvenient or inappropriate time
def_zh: inopportune moment, inopportune interruption, inopportune arrival
quote_en: His arrival was inopportune, interrupting a private conversation that could not be resumed.
quote_zh: "Connelly had been interrupted while taking his pleasure with Gloria, his current wench." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead shows that for the enslaved, there is no opportune time; any moment of apparent peace is always already threatened by the possibility of violence, making every celebration inopportune.
ex_zh: The novel's use of festivals and holidays as settings for violence suggests that the masters deliberately choose inopportune moments to maximize the psychological impact, turning joy into dread.

def_en: to groom or adorn oneself; to show self-satisfaction
def_zh: preen oneself, preen before a mirror, preen with pride
quote_en: The actor preened before the mirror, adjusting his collar for the fifth time.
quote_zh: "Nag was haughty for a nigger gal even before the overseer bestowed his slim favors upon her, preening, gloating over the ill treatment that she alone escaped." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "preen" to expose the fragility of the slave's temporary advantage: those who preen over their favored status are setting themselves up for a fall.
ex_zh: The novel's treatment of Nag's preening and subsequent humiliation shows how the plantation system creates false hierarchies among the enslaved, turning them against each other to prevent solidarity.

def_en: a settling of accounts; a time of punishment or reward
def_zh: day of reckoning, final reckoning, moral reckoning
quote_en: The corrupt official knew that a reckoning was coming, but he continued his schemes anyway.
quote_zh: "Longtime residents of the Randall plantation couldn't help but compare the funerals of the father and the son." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "reckoning" to describe moments when a society confronts its foundational contradictions, usually choosing violence over resolution.
ex_zh: The novel suggests that America has never truly had its reckoning with slavery; instead, each generation produces new forms of racial control that postpone the final account.

def_en: lacking a plan, purpose, or enthusiasm; disconnected
def_zh: desultory conversation, desultory effort, desultory reading
quote_en: The students made a desultory effort on their final project, clearly exhausted from the semester.
quote_zh: "The northern and southern halves of the great Randall plantation exchanged slaves all the time, unloading beat niggers, skulky workers, and rascals on each other in a desultory game." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "desultory" to capture the casual cruelty of the plantation system, where the movement of human beings is treated as an afterthought.
ex_zh: The novel's desultory structure—moving from one state to another without a clear linear progression—mirrors the randomness with which slaves were bought, sold, and traded.

def_en: a group of people summoned by a sheriff to enforce the law; any armed group
def_zh: posse comitatus, posse of deputies, form a posse
quote_en: The sheriff assembled a posse to track the fugitives through the mountains.
quote_zh: "Patrollers and posses of low whites harassed and bullied. The quarters of all the nearby plantations were thoroughly searched." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "posse" to demystify law enforcement in the slave states, showing it as a gang of violent men whose authority comes not from law but from terror.
ex_zh: The novel repeatedly shows posses as instruments of white solidarity across class lines: poor whites eagerly join to hunt black bodies, revealing how racism unites the white population.

def_en: to search for and collect usable items from waste; to feed on carrion
def_zh: scavenge for food, scavenge scraps, scavenge through trash
quote_en: The refugees scavenged for food among the ruins of their former homes.
quote_zh: "A week passed before the slaves hunted and scavenged in the swamp again. That's where the food was." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "scavenge" to describe the economic position of the enslaved: they are permitted to gather the leftovers of the plantation economy.
ex_zh: The novel shows scavenging as both a literal survival activity and a metaphor for the way the enslaved must build lives from the discarded remnants of the master's world.

def_en: a person who has escaped from captivity or is in hiding
def_zh: fugitive from justice, fugitive slave, fugitive hunter
quote_en: The police set up roadblocks to capture the fugitive who escaped from prison.
quote_zh: "No one had escaped the Randall plantation before. The fugitives were always clawed back." (Georgia)
ex_en: The fugitive's journey transforms from a physical escape into a psychological reckoning with America's original sin, suggesting that freedom is not a destination but an endless flight from inherited trauma.
ex_zh: Whitehead's use of the word "fugitive" refuses the legalistic term "runaway slave," rehumanizing Cora by emphasizing her agency in flight rather than her status as stolen property.

def_en: a person's appearance or manner, especially as an indication of character
def_zh: dignified mien, somber mien, commanding mien
quote_en: The general had a commanding mien that inspired loyalty in his troops.
quote_zh: "Ridgeway was six and a half feet tall, with the square face and thick neck of a hammer. He maintained a serene comportment at all times but generated a threatening atmosphere." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "mien" to explore the performance of power: Ridgeway's calm exterior is not genuine serenity but a tool of intimidation.
ex_zh: The novel contrasts the mien of various authority figures (Ridgeway, Terrance, the night riders) to show that the slave system depends on a theatrical repertoire of faces.

def_en: a person who supports or agrees with a particular cause or group
def_zh: rebel sympathizer, abolitionist sympathizer, secret sympathizer
quote_en: The government arrested anyone suspected of being a rebel sympathizer.
quote_zh: "The sympathizers would be rooted out and tarred and feathered, Ridgeway assured his host." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead complicates the figure of the white sympathizer by showing that even well-intentioned allies are limited by their own positionality.
ex_zh: The novel's treatment of Fletcher, Sam, and Martin shows that sympathizers are often motivated by a mix of genuine conviction and personal guilt.

def_en: to sail or travel all the way around something; to avoid
def_zh: circumnavigate the globe, circumnavigate an obstacle, circumnavigate a problem
quote_en: The explorer spent three years circumnavigating the globe.
quote_zh: "On horses they circumnavigated the fields, comparing the progress of the harvest on the two halves." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "circumnavigate" to describe the masters' relationship to the enslaved: they circle around them, inspect them, quantify them, but never see them as human.
ex_zh: The novel's structure, which takes Cora on a geographical circumnavigation of the South, suggests that escaping the plantation requires not just movement but a complete reorientation of how one sees the land.

def_en: a person who is guided and supported by an older or more experienced person
def_zh: mentor and protégé, young protégé, musical protégé
quote_en: The famous sculptor took the young artist as his protégé, teaching her everything he knew.
quote_zh: "By the time of James's passing, the Randall sons had cut off social ties with their father's peers and protégés." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead shows that the master-protégé relationship is impossible under slavery because genuine mentorship requires recognizing the humanity of the other.
ex_zh: The novel contrasts the false protégé relationship (Terrance and his favorites) with the genuine one (Royal guiding Cora), suggesting that freedom is learned not from masters but from fellow fugitives.

def_en: a secret meeting, especially between lovers
def_zh: clandestine assignation, assignation with a lover, assignation spot
quote_en: The novel begins with a clandestine assignation in a rain-soaked garden.
quote_zh: "The rotting schoolhouse was used for assignations and all those different lessons." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "assignation" to capture the furtive, stolen quality of slave intimacy, where even private meetings are subject to interruption and punishment.
ex_zh: The novel's treatment of assignations reveals that the plantation cannot fully control the bodies of the enslaved, even as it claims total ownership.

def_en: respected and impressive; majestic
def_zh: august body, august presence, august occasion
quote_en: The august assembly of world leaders gathered to sign the treaty.
quote_zh: "A band of august souls from Atlanta and Savannah arrived in a carriage to witness Big Anthony's torture." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "august" ironically to describe the white elites whose dignity is built on the suffering of the enslaved.
ex_zh: The novel forces readers to see that the august institutions of the Old South—the legislature, the judiciary, the church—were all complicit in the torture of human beings.

def_en: a person who supported the end of slavery
def_zh: radical abolitionist, abolitionist movement, abolitionist literature
quote_en: Frederick Douglass was the most famous abolitionist of his era.
quote_zh: "He had never been active in abolitionist circles up north but observing the monstrous system firsthand gave him thoughts he did not recognize." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead presents abolitionists as a mixed group: some are genuine heroes, others are motivated by guilt or self-interest, but all are essential to the underground railroad.
ex_zh: The novel suggests that true abolitionism requires not just intellectual conviction but direct action—Fletcher, Sam, and Martin all risk their lives because they have seen slavery firsthand.

def_en: truthfulness; accuracy
def_zh: question the veracity, veracity of the claim, veracity of testimony
quote_en: The journalist questioned the veracity of the government's official statement.
quote_zh: "He had met this sort of white man before, earnest and believing what came out of their mouths. The veracity of their words was another matter, but at least they believed them." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "veracity" to explore the unreliability of testimony in a slave society, where even well-meaning whites may be blind to their own complicity.
ex_zh: The novel's narrative voice, which shifts between omniscient third-person and limited perspectives, constantly questions the veracity of any single account.

def_en: to go or travel along a winding course
def_zh: wend one's way, wend through the crowd, wend homeward
quote_en: The pilgrims wended their way through the narrow streets toward the cathedral.
quote_zh: "The three of them wended west, tromping through the black water." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "wend" to evoke the slow, uncertain, meandering quality of escape—the underground railroad is not a straight line but a winding path through dangerous territory.
ex_zh: The novel's structure wends through different states and narrative modes, refusing a simple linear progression, just as Cora's escape wends through swamps, tunnels, and false promises.

def_en: the object of a hunt; a large animal being hunted
def_zh: pursue one's quarry, elusive quarry, hunter and quarry
quote_en: The hounds pursued their quarry through the dense underbrush.
quote_zh: "The hog hunters waited for their quarry, which turned nocturnal in the hot weather. The runaways were a different sort of beast but more remunerative." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "quarry" to blur the line between human and animal, showing how the slave system classifies fugitives as game to be hunted.
ex_zh: The novel's many hunting scenes force readers to see the fugitive as quarry, but also to recognize that the hunters are the true beasts.

def_en: financially rewarding; profitable
def_zh: remunerative employment, remunerative venture, highly remunerative
quote_en: She left her teaching position for a more remunerative career in finance.
quote_zh: "The runaways were a different sort of beast but more remunerative." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "remunerative" to expose the economic logic that drives slave catching: human beings are a source of profit.
ex_zh: The novel suggests that the entire slave system is remunerative for some, but that profit is built on the commodification of human life—a calculation that deforms everyone involved.

def_en: something that is imagined or invented
def_zh: figment of the imagination, mere figment, figment and fantasy
quote_en: The monster turned out to be a figment of the child's imagination.
quote_zh: "Time was a figment. Caesar called her name, pulling her up." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "figment" to suggest that the normal measurements of reality (time, space, identity) become unreliable in the fugitive's experience.
ex_zh: The novel's treatment of memory and trauma shows that the past is not a figment but a living presence—Ajarry's experience lives on in Cora.

def_en: to align or position something relative to a direction
def_zh: orient oneself, orient toward, orient around
quote_en: She needed a few days to orient herself in the new city.
quote_zh: "He oriented himself with the constellations and the runaways stumbled on, impelled into the night." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "orient" to emphasize the importance of knowing one's position—the fugitive must constantly reorient, as the landmarks of the plantation are replaced by the stars.
ex_zh: The novel suggests that freedom requires a complete reorientation of one's moral compass: the values of the plantation must be abandoned for the fugitive's values of resistance and flight.

def_en: to roam in search of plunder or goods to steal
def_zh: marauding band, maraud through, maraud and pillage
quote_en: Barbarian tribes marauded across the countryside, burning villages in their path.
quote_zh: "Patrol bands marauded through the slave villages and ransacked the homes of freemen, stealing and committing assaults." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "maraud" to describe state-sanctioned violence, showing that the line between lawful authority and criminal gang is thin in a slave society.
ex_zh: The novel's marauding posses and patrollers reveal that the slave system cannot maintain order through law alone; it requires periodic outbursts of uncontrolled violence.

def_en: the protective care of God or nature; timely preparation
def_zh: divine providence, act of providence, trust in providence
quote_en: By providence, the ship arrived just before the hurricane struck.
quote_zh: "Providence smiled on the fugitives: The hunters believed they hid in the swamp." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "providence" ironically: the fugitives succeed not because of divine intervention but because of human error and their own ingenuity.
ex_zh: The novel questions the role of providence in the slave's escape: if God is just, why does he not help all fugitives? The answer seems to be that the underground railroad is a human, not divine, creation.

def_en: a metal band for restraining the hands; to confine
def_zh: manacle and chain, manacle the wrists, manacle of convention
quote_en: The guards manacled the prisoner's wrists behind his back.
quote_zh: "She saw the chains first. Thousands of them dangled off the wall on nails in a morbid inventory of manacles and fetters." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "manacle" as a synecdoche for the entire slave system: the small metal device represents the massive machinery of control.
ex_zh: The novel's description of the manacle collection in Lumbly's barn is a museum of suffering, each set of irons a story of capture and confinement.

def_en: to leave hurriedly and secretly, typically to escape from custody
def_zh: abscond with funds, abscond from justice, abscond into the night
quote_en: The accountant absconded with millions of dollars from the company.
quote_zh: "Mabel had packed for her adventure... She absconded from her plantation in the confusion of the reorganization." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "abscond" to emphasize the secrecy and speed required for escape, but also the desperation: most absconding slaves are caught.
ex_zh: The novel's treatment of absconding shows that the decision to run is not a calculation of success but a refusal to continue accepting the unacceptable—even a failed abscond is an assertion of humanity.

def_en: a characteristic rising and falling rhythm; to speak in a cheerful or musical manner
def_zh: Irish lilt, voice lilt, melodic lilt
quote_en: Her voice had a gentle Irish lilt that made everyone feel at ease.
quote_zh: "He had an odd manner of speech, a queer lilt that reminded Cora of the way those on the plantation sounded after they lost their wits." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "lilt" to suggest that the underground railroad attracts eccentric, marginal figures whose strangeness is a form of resistance.
ex_zh: The novel's cast of characters (Lumbly, Sam, Homer, Royal) all have a certain lilt—a way of speaking that refuses to fit into the categories of master or slave.

def_en: a benefit or privilege received in addition to regular payment
def_zh: executive perquisites, perquisite of office, perquisite and privilege
quote_en: The company car was a perquisite of the executive position.
quote_zh: "There were perquisites, apart from the thrill of shaming a man in front of his family or roughing up an unseasoned buck who squinted at you the wrong way." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "perquisite" to expose the corruption of the slave-catching system: the patrollers are not disinterested enforcers of law but predators who use their authority for personal gain.
ex_zh: The novel's perquisites—whiskey, women, violence—reveal that the slave system rewards the worst instincts of the poor white population, turning them into willing tools of the planter class.

def_en: outstandingly bad; shocking
def_zh: egregious error, egregious violation, egregious behavior
quote_en: The referee made an egregious error that cost the team the championship.
quote_zh: "The other patrollers were boys and men of bad character; the work attracted a type. In another country they would have been criminals, but this was America." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "egregious" to describe acts so terrible that they stand out even in a system built on routine cruelty—Ridgeway's relative restraint is not virtue but calculation.
ex_zh: The novel suggests that the most egregious acts of violence are not the exceptions but the logical conclusions of a system that treats human beings as property.

def_en: a miserly person who avoids spending money
def_zh: penny-pinching skinflint, old skinflint, skinflint and miser
quote_en: The old skinflint refused to donate even a single dollar to the charity.
quote_zh: "An absconded slave might fetch as little as two dollars if the owner was a skinflint or the nigger was busted, and as much as a hundred dollars." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "skinflint" to show that even the miserliness of masters affects the lives of the enslaved—a stingy master pays lower rewards, making fugitives less valuable and more likely to be killed.
ex_zh: The novel's economic language (skinflint, liquidate, remunerative) reveals that the slave system is a market like any other, with supply and demand determining the value of human life.

def_en: obtrusively bright and showy; lurid
def_zh: garish colors, garish display, garish and gaudy
quote_en: The casino's garish neon lights flashed through the night.
quote_zh: "Even the shortest ferry ride delivered him to a new island nation, garish and imposing, each town more lunatic and complicated than the last." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "garish" to describe the North as seen through Ridgeway's hostile eyes—its colors are too bright, its freedoms too loud, its complexity an affront to his orderly vision of racial hierarchy.
ex_zh: The novel's treatment of the North as garish suggests that freedom can be disorienting, even ugly, and that the line between free states and slave states is not as clear as the fugitives hope.

def_en: a rich fabric woven with raised patterns, often metallic thread
def_zh: gold brocade, brocade fabric, brocade gown
quote_en: The queen's gown was made of gold brocade and trimmed with ermine.
quote_zh: "The twenty dollars went to new boots and a brocade coat like he'd seen some worthies wear in D.C. He wore the boots for many years. His belly outgrew the coat sooner than that." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "brocade" to symbolize the aspirational costume of the slave catcher, who dresses like a gentleman but remains a killer—the fine fabric cannot disguise his brutality.
ex_zh: The novel's attention to clothing (brocade coats, silk tobacco pouches, negro cloth) shows how material culture reflects and reinforces social hierarchies.

def_en: enormous; of immense size
def_zh: gargantuan task, gargantuan appetite, gargantuan scale
quote_en: The project required a gargantuan effort from the entire team.
quote_zh: "New York was the start of a wild time. Ridgeway worked retrieval... New York became a frequent destination, and after exploring new aspects of his character, Ridgeway picked up stakes. Up north, the gargantuan metropolis, the liberty movement, and the ingenuity of the colored community all converged to portray the true scale of the hunt." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "gargantuan" to convey the overwhelming size and complexity of the North, which is both a refuge and a labyrinth where fugitives can get lost.
ex_zh: The novel's gargantuan cities (New York, Philadelphia, Boston) are presented as alternatives to the plantation, but they are also sites of capture, as Ridgeway's success there shows—size does not guarantee safety.

def_en: a person employed to load and unload ships
def_zh: dock stevedore, stevedore labor, stevedore union
quote_en: The stevedores worked through the night to unload the cargo from Africa.
quote_zh: "Sympathizers and mercenary captains smuggled fugitives into the city ports. In turn, stevedores and dockhands and clerks furnished him with information and he scooped up the rascals on the threshold of deliverance." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "stevedore" to show that the underground railroad depends on ordinary working people, whose daily labor makes escape possible—but also that some of those workers betray fugitives for profit.
ex_zh: The novel's stevedores are like the patrollers: they occupy a liminal space between the enslaved and the free, and their choices determine whether a fugitive reaches freedom or is dragged back.

def_en: the act of being rescued or set free
def_zh: prayer for deliverance, deliverance from evil, day of deliverance
quote_en: The hostages prayed for deliverance from their captors.
quote_zh: "Stevedores and dockhands and clerks furnished him with information and he scooped up the rascals on the threshold of deliverance." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "deliverance" to name the fugitive's goal, but also to emphasize its fragility: deliverance is never guaranteed, and the moment of rescue is also the moment of greatest danger.
ex_zh: The novel's many near-deliverances (Cora in South Carolina, Cora in the attic, Cora on the train) create a pattern of hope and disappointment that mirrors the historical experience of the underground railroad.

def_en: an opening strategy designed to gain an advantage
def_zh: opening gambit, chess gambit, strategic gambit
quote_en: Her sudden question was a gambit to catch him off guard.
quote_zh: "High-minded idiocy pitted against the power of coin. For a gratuity, the city recorder tipped him to freshly jailed fugitives and hurriedly signed them over for release. They'd be halfway through New Jersey before the abolitionists had even gotten out of bed." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "gambit" to describe the tactical moves in the cat-and-mouse game between abolitionists and slave catchers—each side has its gambits, and the fugitives are the pieces being moved.
ex_zh: The novel suggests that the underground railroad is a kind of chess game with gambits and counter-gambits, but the stakes are human lives, and the players are not evenly matched.

def_en: the entrails and internal organs of an animal used as food; refuse
def_zh: offal and refuse, meat offal, offal disposal
quote_en: The butcher sold the offal to specialty markets at a low price.
quote_zh: "The gutters of the city overflowed with offal and refuse—but the mess found its place in time. The same laws governed garbage and people." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "offal" to draw a parallel between the treatment of garbage and the treatment of people: both are swept out of sight, both are considered waste, and both are subject to the same economic laws.
ex_zh: The novel's offal imagery (the gutters, the swamps, the burning bodies) suggests that America's treatment of its unwanted populations follows the same logic: they are offal to be disposed of.

def_en: the undermining of authority or established systems
def_zh: subversion of authority, subversion of order, subversion and sedition
quote_en: The government accused the newspaper of publishing materials that encouraged subversion.
quote_zh: "The underground railroad maintained no lines to speak of. The decoys in negro dress, the secret codes in the back pages of newspapers. They openly bragged of their subversion, hustling a slave out the back door as the slave catchers broke down the front." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "subversion" to name the underground railroad's project: not just helping individuals escape but undermining the entire system of slavery by proving it can be defeated.
ex_zh: The novel's subversive structure—turning the railroad, a symbol of American progress, into a secret network of escape—suggests that true subversion comes from reimagining the nation's founding myths.

def_en: a refutation or contradiction; a counterargument
def_zh: strong rebuttal, legal rebuttal, rebuttal evidence
quote_en: The scientist published a detailed rebuttal to the study's flawed methodology.
quote_zh: "The abolitionist wing of the Methodists circulated his pamphlets on Sunday morning and London periodicals published his arguments without rebuttal." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "rebuttal" to describe the rhetorical battle over slavery: the abolitionists offer moral rebuttals, the slaveholders offer violence, and the two never truly meet.
ex_zh: The novel suggests that no rebuttal, however eloquent, can defeat the slave system because the system is not based on argument but on force; the only effective rebuttal is escape.

def_en: a decorative molding at the top of a building or wall
def_zh: white cornice, cornice of a building, carved cornice
quote_en: The ancient temple's marble cornice had been worn smooth by centuries of rain.
quote_zh: "She could just make out the top two floors of the Griffin Building, with its white cornices that blazed in the sunlight." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "cornice" as a symbol of the false promise of South Carolina's colored uplift program: the beautiful building hides the sterilization experiments inside.
ex_zh: The novel's architectural details (cornices, dormitories, the Griffin Building) show how the built environment reflects and reinforces racial hierarchies.

def_en: decorated with ruffles or fringes; overly ornate
def_zh: frilly dress, frilly decoration, frilly and feminine
quote_en: The little girl wore a frilly pink dress to the wedding.
quote_zh: "The dressmaker with her frilly, colorful creations draped on hooped wire." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "frilly" to represent the consumer goods that define freedom for Bessie in South Carolina—pretty things that distract her from the fact that her body is still controlled by the state.
ex_zh: The novel's treatment of frilly dresses and other luxury items suggests that consumer culture can be a form of captivity, luring the newly free into debt and dependency.

def_en: a large retail store selling a wide variety of goods
def_zh: department emporium, emporium of goods, shopping emporium
quote_en: The old emporium on Main Street sold everything from farm tools to candy.
quote_zh: "The overstuffed emporiums and their wonderland of goods, the rival general stores on either side of Main Street." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "emporium" to represent the seductive abundance of consumer capitalism, which promises freedom through purchase but actually creates new forms of dependence.
ex_zh: The novel's emporiums are the commercial counterpart to the museum: both display goods and people, both arrange the world for white consumption.

def_en: a densely populated or maze-like area
def_zh: rabbit warren, warren of alleys, warren of offices
quote_en: The old city was a warren of narrow alleys and hidden courtyards.
quote_zh: "Mr. Anderson's floor was a warren of lawyer's offices, with rich carpets, walls of dark brown wood, and doors inlaid with frosted glass." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "warren" to describe the spaces where power is exercised: hidden, maze-like, inaccessible to the people whose lives are decided there.
ex_zh: The novel's warrens (the Griffin Building, the underground tunnels, the plantation quarters) are all spaces where the enslaved or the poor are excluded.

def_en: the customers or patrons of a business
def_zh: loyal clientele, upscale clientele, clientele base
quote_en: The exclusive restaurant attracted a wealthy clientele from across the city.
quote_zh: "She crossed the street to avoid the jumble of saloons and their disreputable clientele." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "clientele" to show how public spaces are segregated by class and race, and how the clientele of a saloon can be a threat to any black woman who passes by.
ex_zh: The novel's treatment of clientele suggests that freedom of movement is not guaranteed by law but by the willingness of the white clientele to tolerate your presence.

def_en: wild, untamed, or having returned to a wild state
def_zh: feral child, feral animal, feral gaze
quote_en: The abandoned cat had become feral, hissing at anyone who approached.
quote_zh: "The boy's shining eyes, set in his round pudgy face, were at once feral and serene. A kindred spirit." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "feral" to describe Homer, the strange black boy who serves Ridgeway—he is wild, untamed, but also eerily calm, as if he has transcended the normal categories of human emotion.
ex_zh: The novel's feral characters (Homer, the Hob women, the maroons) exist outside the master-slave binary, inhabiting a liminal space that is both dangerous and free.

def_en: not spoiled or stained; pure
def_zh: unsullied reputation, unsullied virtue, unsullied white
quote_en: The mountain snow remained unsullied by the city's pollution.
quote_zh: "The brick was a pure, unsullied color, without so much as a dot of mud splashed from the rain." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "unsullied" to describe the South Carolina dormitories' appearance, which is a lie—the unsullied brick hides the unspeakable experiments conducted inside.
ex_zh: The novel's attention to surfaces that appear unsullied (whitewashed cabins, clean dormitories, neat towns) reveals that the slave system invests enormous energy in maintaining the appearance of order and purity.

def_en: to slow down or reduce speed
def_zh: decelerate quickly, decelerate to a stop, decelerate the vehicle
quote_en: The train began to decelerate as it approached the station.
quote_zh: "Then the locomotive decelerated. Caesar jumped up. They could scarcely believe it." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "decelerate" to describe the rhythm of the underground railroad, where moments of rest are always followed by renewed flight—the fugitive can never stop decelerating, never settle.
ex_zh: The novel's structure of acceleration and deceleration mirrors the fugitive's psychological state: the terror of the chase, the brief respite of a station, the dread of the next departure.

def_en: destruction or ruin; to cause suffering
def_zh: wrack and ruin, wracked with pain, wracked by guilt
quote_en: The coastal town was left in wrack after the hurricane passed.
quote_zh: "The engineer mopped sweat and soot from his face and was about to speak when a ferocious coughing wracked his person." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "wrack" to describe the physical toll of the underground railroad on its workers—their bodies are wracked by disease, exhaustion, and the constant terror of discovery.
ex_zh: The novel's engineers, station agents, and conductors are all wracked by the work they do, but they continue because the alternative—allowing the slave system to continue unchallenged—is worse.

def_en: food or provisions
def_zh: victuals and supplies, cook victuals, store victuals
quote_en: The travelers packed enough victuals to last the entire journey.
quote_zh: "The basket contained victuals: bread, half a chicken, water, and a bottle of beer. They were so hungry they shook out the crumbs from the basket to divvy." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "victuals" to emphasize the material conditions of escape: fugitives are always hungry, always rationing, always at the edge of starvation—food is as precious as freedom.
ex_zh: The novel's attention to victuals (the yams Cora digs up, the bread in the station baskets) shows that the underground railroad is not just a network of tunnels but a logistical operation.

def_en: to divide or share something
def_zh: divvy up, divvy the spoils, divvy among
quote_en: The thieves agreed to divvy the loot equally among themselves.
quote_zh: "They were so hungry they shook out the crumbs from the basket to divvy." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "divvy" to show how fugitives must share scarce resources, creating a miniature communist economy based on mutual aid and survival.
ex_zh: The novel's divvying scenes contrast with the plantation's distribution of rations, where the master decides who gets what—the fugitives' divvying is an act of self-determination.

def_en: a small detail or point of law
def_zh: legal technicality, technicality of the law, on a technicality
quote_en: The case was dismissed on a technicality, not on its merits.
quote_zh: "'It says here we're the property of the United States Government,' Caesar pointed out. 'That's a technicality,' Sam said." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "technicality" to expose the legal fictions that underpin the slave system—the claim that the government owns the colored population is a technicality that can be ignored when convenient.
ex_zh: The novel's many legal technicalities (the Fugitive Slave Law, the bills of sale, the emancipation papers) show that the law is not a neutral arbiter of justice but a tool of oppression.

def_en: a woman with whom secrets are shared
def_zh: trusted confidante, close confidante, confidante and advisor
quote_en: The queen's only confidante was her younger sister.
quote_zh: "A confidante in the Griffin Building had forged these papers for them." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "confidante" to highlight the importance of trust in the underground railroad: the entire network depends on confidantes who keep secrets, but every confidante is also a potential informant.
ex_zh: The novel's treatment of confidantes (Miss Lucy, the Anderson's maid, Fiona) shows that intimacy and betrayal are two sides of the same coin in a slave society.

def_en: an elementary textbook for teaching children to read
def_zh: reading primer, primer for beginners, primer lesson
quote_en: The child struggled through the first pages of her primer, sounding out each letter.
quote_zh: "She removed her primer from her trunk. While the other girls gossiped and said good night one by one, Cora practiced her letters." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "primer" to symbolize the education that the slave system forbids—learning to read is an act of rebellion, and the primer is a weapon in the struggle for freedom.
ex_zh: The novel's primers (Cora's, the children's at the Valentine farm, the library books) represent the promise of self-improvement and liberation through literacy.

def_en: patient self-control; tolerance
def_zh: forbearance and patience, exercise forbearance, judicial forbearance
quote_en: The teacher showed great forbearance with her most difficult students.
quote_zh: "The families were told to exercise forbearance with inexperienced help." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "forbearance" to describe the attitude white employers are asked to adopt toward their colored servants—forbearance is a form of paternalism that still assumes the superiority of the white person.
ex_zh: The novel suggests that forbearance is not kindness but a strategy of control: the master's forbearance can be withdrawn at any moment.

def_en: to groom oneself with elaborate care
def_zh: primp before a mirror, primp and preen, primp for a date
quote_en: She spent an hour primping before the party, checking her hair from every angle.
quote_zh: "The girls in the bunk room were in a state of great excitement over the evening's gathering, primping before the small mirror." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "primp" to show how the colored women of South Carolina try to assert their humanity through appearance, even as their bodies are subject to state control.
ex_zh: The novel's primping scenes are poignant because the readers know that the women's efforts to look beautiful will not protect them from the doctors' scalpels.

def_en: an uncouth or aggressive man
def_zh: drunken lout, lout and boor, loutish behavior
quote_en: The drunken lout was asked to leave the tavern after shouting at the bartender.
quote_zh: "One of the louts at the tavern gave him a black eye. He's proud of it. Says he'd always wanted one." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "lout" to describe the kind of men who become patrollers and slave catchers—not the planters, but the poor whites whose brutality is their only form of power.
ex_zh: The novel's louts (the hog hunters, the night riders, the posse members) are the foot soldiers of the slave system, men whose violence is rewarded.

def_en: having developed certain abilities at an earlier age than usual
def_zh: precocious child, precocious talent, precocious intelligence
quote_en: The precocious child was reading novels by the age of seven.
quote_zh: "He demonstrated his brilliance from an early age, a sickly child who made the family library his playground, poring over volumes he struggled to lift from the shelves." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "precocious" to describe Lander, whose early genius is both a gift and a burden—he sees the world more clearly than others, but that clarity cannot save him from the bullet.
ex_zh: The novel's precocious characters (Lander, Homer, Chester) are all marked by their intelligence, but their precocity does not protect them from the violence of the slave system.

def_en: required by law or rules; compulsory
def_zh: mandatory attendance, mandatory requirement, mandatory minimum
quote_en: Wearing a seatbelt is mandatory in most countries.
quote_zh: "As of this week, it is mandatory for some in the state. Colored women who have already birthed more than two children, in the name of population control. Imbeciles and the otherwise mentally unfit, for obvious reasons." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "mandatory" to expose the eugenic logic of South Carolina's colored uplift program: the state decides which bodies are fit to reproduce, and mandatory sterilization is the tool.
ex_zh: The novel's mandatory procedures (the blood tests, the examinations, the surgeries) show that the line between medicine and torture is thin when the patients are black.

def_en: stubbornly disobedient; resistant to authority
def_zh: recalcitrant patient, recalcitrant student, recalcitrant employee
quote_en: The recalcitrant student refused to follow any classroom rules.
quote_zh: "She wasn't his first recalcitrant patient. Dr. Stevens put the matter aside without losing his warm demeanor." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "recalcitrant" to describe the attitude of those who refuse to submit to the state's control over their bodies—recalcitrance is a form of resistance.
ex_zh: The novel's recalcitrant characters (Cora, Caesar, the woman who screams about her babies) are all punished for their refusal to comply, but their recalcitrance is also what makes them fugitives.

def_en: without suffering any injury or damage
def_zh: escape unscathed, emerge unscathed, survive unscathed
quote_en: The driver walked away from the car accident completely unscathed.
quote_zh: "Cora had become too accustomed to escaping unscathed from encounters with white authority." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "unscathed" to question the possibility of escaping the slave system without damage—even if the body is unscathed, the psyche bears scars that never fully heal.
ex_zh: The novel's survivors (Cora, the Valentine farm refugees, the Hob women) are all marked by their experiences, and the idea of emerging unscathed is a fantasy.

def_en: accorded a great deal of respect, especially because of age or wisdom
def_zh: venerable elder, venerable institution, venerable tradition
quote_en: The venerable professor had taught at the university for fifty years.
quote_zh: "The cross-sections of pumpkins and the life rings of venerable white oaks." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "venerable" ironically to describe the natural objects in the museum, but also to comment on the false reverence for white institutions.
ex_zh: The novel's venerable figures (Jockey, Old Abraham) are revered not for wisdom alone but for having survived—in a system designed to kill you, living to old age is an act of heroic resistance.

def_en: a state of disorder or confusion
def_zh: in disarray, thrown into disarray, complete disarray
quote_en: The office was in complete disarray after the late-night party.
quote_zh: "She had been too distracted to notice the house's disarray on her previous visit, the grimed plates, sawdust, and piles of clothes." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "disarray" to describe the personal lives of those who work for the underground railroad—their commitment to the cause leaves no room for order or comfort.
ex_zh: The novel's disarray (Sam's messy cottage, Martin's cluttered attic, the abandoned stations) shows that the fight against slavery is not clean or orderly.

def_en: a strong desire or longing
def_zh: hankering for adventure, hankering after fame, hankering for the past
quote_en: Despite his success, he still had a hankering for the simpler life of his childhood.
quote_zh: "Her first husband developed a hankering for corn whiskey and started using his big hands to make big fists." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "hankering" to describe the desires that lead to self-destruction—the hankering for whiskey destroys the husband, just as the hankering for control destroys the masters.
ex_zh: The novel's hankerings (for whiskey, for freedom, for revenge) are all expressions of lack, and how characters satisfy their hankerings reveals their moral character.

def_en: gloomy, morose, or sullen in temperament
def_zh: saturnine disposition, saturnine expression, saturnine mood
quote_en: The old man had a saturnine disposition that discouraged conversation.
quote_zh: "The doctor preferred Miss Trumball's establishment to the Lanchester House, whose girls had a saturnine disposition in his opinion, as if imported from Maine or other gloom-loving provinces." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "saturnine" to describe the prostitutes whom the doctor rejects, revealing his misogyny and his expectation that women should be cheerful and available.
ex_zh: The novel's saturnine characters (the doctor, Ridgeway, Terrance) project their own gloom onto others, blaming their victims for the darkness in their own souls.

def_en: inclined or susceptible to something beforehand
def_zh: genetically predisposed, predisposed to illness, predisposed to violence
quote_en: Some people are genetically predisposed to certain diseases.
quote_zh: "Did Sam know that the Igbo tribe of the African continent is predisposed to nervous disorders, suicide and black moods?" (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "predisposed" to expose the pseudo-scientific racism that underlies eugenics—the claim that certain races are predisposed to certain traits is used to justify their elimination.
ex_zh: The novel's treatment of predisposition shows how science can be corrupted to serve the interests of power, turning human beings into data points.

def_en: feeling or expressing pensive sadness
def_zh: melancholic mood, melancholic temperament, melancholic music
quote_en: The melancholic painting depicted a lone figure gazing at a distant sea.
quote_zh: "What if we performed adjustments to the niggers' breeding patterns and removed those of melancholic tendency?" (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "melancholic" to show how the state pathologizes normal human emotions in the colored population—sadness is not a response to oppression but a trait to be bred out.
ex_zh: The novel's melancholic characters (the Hob women, the woman who screams about her babies, the victims of sterilization) are not suffering from a genetic predisposition but from real trauma.

def_en: capable of being transmitted from one person to another
def_zh: communicable disease, communicable illness, communicable infection
quote_en: Measles is a highly communicable disease that spreads through respiratory droplets.
quote_zh: "Controlled sterilization, research into communicable diseases, the perfection of new surgical techniques on the socially unfit—was it any wonder the best medical talents in the country were flocking to South Carolina?" (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "communicable" to describe the diseases that the state uses as justification for medical experimentation on the colored population—the research is framed as public health, but it is really eugenics.
ex_zh: The novel's communicable diseases (syphilis, yellow fever, the plague) are all used as excuses to violate the bodies of the enslaved.

def_en: not able to be corrected or reformed
def_zh: incorrigible criminal, incorrigible behavior, incorrigible optimist
quote_en: The judge sentenced the incorrigible youth to a reformatory for repeat offenders.
quote_zh: "Drunkards, incorrigibles, poor whites who didn't even own shoes delighted in this opportunity to scourge the colored population." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "incorrigible" to describe the poor whites who become patrollers—they are considered incorrigible by respectable society, but their brutality is useful to the planters.
ex_zh: The novel's incorrigibles (the hog hunters, the night riders, the louts) are the disposable enforcers of the slave system, men whose incorrigibility is tolerated because it serves the interests of power.

def_en: strong hostility or ill feeling
def_zh: personal animus, racial animus, animus toward
quote_en: The debate was conducted without personal animus, despite the strong disagreements.
quote_zh: "Cora didn't know the source of her animus, or care. The woman irked her. Cora stared into her eyes, unwavering and fierce, until the woman broke." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "animus" to describe the irrational hatred that white people direct at the colored population—an animus that is not based on anything the individual has done but on the color of their skin.
ex_zh: The novel's animus (the red-haired woman, the night riders, the mob in North Carolina) is not personal but systemic—it is the hatred that the slave system breeds.

def_en: feeling strained and harassed; troubled
def_zh: harried mother, harried clerk, harried expression
quote_en: The harried mother tried to calm her three crying children while answering the phone.
quote_zh: "Cora selected one patron per hour to evil-eye—a young clerk, a harried matron, a sour youth who liked to batter the glass." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "harried" to describe the white museum patrons whose own anxieties make them vulnerable to Cora's stare—their harried lives are nothing compared to hers.
ex_zh: The novel's harried characters (the matron, the clerk, the museum visitors) are all caught up in the machinery of white supremacy, and their harried state reveals that the system deforms the lives of those who benefit from it.

def_en: a tight grasp; a group or set
def_zh: clutch of children, clutch of eggs, clutch at straws
quote_en: The mother clutched her child's hand as they crossed the busy street.
quote_zh: "A harried matron corralling an unruly clutch of children—she was one of the weak links Cora selected." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "clutch" to describe a group of children, but also to evoke the grasping, controlling nature of white motherhood—the matron clutches her children as if they are possessions.
ex_zh: The novel's clutches (of children, of slaves, of fugitives) all involve the exercise of power, and the act of clutching is always ambiguous—it can be protective or possessive.

def_en: a small, showy trinket or decoration
def_zh: pretty bauble, shiny bauble, worthless bauble
quote_en: The Christmas tree was covered with colorful baubles and tinsel.
quote_zh: "Cora held the idea of freedom up to the light like a hideous bauble, tilting it so—was it real, or merely a pretty thing that would shatter when she tried to grasp it?" (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "bauble" to question the reality of freedom—is it a solid thing or just a pretty bauble that will break when you try to use it? The novel's answer is ambiguous.
ex_zh: The novel's baubles (the promise of South Carolina, the museum's exhibits, the Friday night festivals) are all hollow, shiny surfaces that conceal the violence underneath.

def_en: to laugh in a noisy, gleeful way
def_zh: chortle with glee, chortle at a joke, chortle loudly
quote_en: The children chortled at the clown's silly antics.
quote_zh: "'More likely he had collected last night's dregs at the saloon,' and Cobb and Carpenter chortled." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "chortle" to describe the grave robbers' laughter at their own cruelty, showing how the degradation of the dead becomes a source of amusement for those who profit from it.
ex_zh: The novel's chortles (Cobb and Carpenter, the white audience at the coon show, the gawkers at the auction) are all sounds of callousness, the laughter of those who have lost their capacity for empathy.

def_en: happening in the opposite way to what is expected
def_zh: ironic twist, ironic comment, ironic humor
quote_en: It was ironic that the fire station burned down while the firefighters were away at a training exercise.
quote_zh: "The city had started hanging grave robbers lately—which was ironic or fitting depending on one's perspective, as the bodies of the hanged were given to medical schools for dissection." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "ironic" to describe the relationship between punishment and profit—the state hangs grave robbers, then gives their bodies to the medical schools, creating a closed loop of exploitation.
ex_zh: The novel's ironies (the underground railroad being literally a railroad, the "free" colored people of South Carolina being sterilized, the Freedom Trail lined with corpses) all point to the central irony of America: a nation founded on liberty that depends on slavery.

def_en: a dead body, especially one used for medical dissection
def_zh: human cadaver, cadaver dissection, cadaver lab
quote_en: Medical students studied human anatomy using cadavers donated to the university.
quote_zh: "In death the negro became a human being—only then was he the white man's equal, the cadaver on the dissection table a miracle in Stevens's view." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "cadaver" to expose the terrible irony of medical science: the black body is only valuable when it is dead and can be cut up for the benefit of white students.
ex_zh: The novel's cadavers (the bodies in the medical school, the corpses on the Freedom Trail, the remains of the lynched) are the raw material of white progress—their deaths are the foundation upon which American medicine is built.

def_en: the act of cutting apart a body for scientific examination
def_zh: anatomical dissection, surgical dissection, dissection table
quote_en: The biology class performed a frog dissection to learn about amphibian anatomy.
quote_zh: "Stevens's first anatomy course required two complete dissections—how was he supposed to pay for that? The cost of learning the body's secrets was measured not in dollars alone but in the dignity of the dead." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "dissection" to represent the violence of medical science, which treats the dead as objects to be cut apart for knowledge—a knowledge that is then used to control the living.
ex_zh: The novel's dissections (of cadavers, of the enslaved in medical experiments, of the social body by the state) all involve cutting something open to see how it works, but the thing being cut is never asked for permission.

def_en: to steal things of little value
def_zh: pilfer supplies, pilfer from the till, pilfer and steal
quote_en: The clerk was caught pilfering office supplies from the stockroom.
quote_zh: "Leave a prospect to too late in the evening and you were liable to discover someone else had pilfered the body first—the grave-robbing trade was competitive." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "pilfer" to describe the theft of corpses, showing how even the dead are not safe from the commodification that defines American life—everything, even a body, can be stolen and sold.
ex_zh: The novel's pilfering (of bodies, of food, of freedom) reveals that in a slave society, property is everything and personhood is nothing.

def_en: a cunning plan or action designed to gain an advantage
def_zh: clever ploy, advertising ploy, ploy to distract
quote_en: The discount was just a ploy to attract customers into the store.
quote_zh: "In his glory days, Carpenter elevated the ploys and chicanery of his trade to a devilish art, selling the same cadaver to multiple schools." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "ploy" to describe the grave robbers' schemes, showing that criminal ingenuity can be as sophisticated as any legal enterprise—the ploy is the same whether you are selling cadavers or cotton.
ex_zh: The novel's ploys (Carpenter's schemes, Ridgeway's traps, the underground railroad's deceptions) all involve trickery, and the line between criminal and heroic ploy is drawn not by method but by purpose.

def_en: the use of trickery or deception to achieve a purpose
def_zh: legal chicanery, political chicanery, chicanery and fraud
quote_en: The election was marred by accusations of chicanery and voter fraud.
quote_zh: "Midnight meetings, riverbank chicanery, intrigue at the crossroads—Donald's many work trips during Martin's childhood were in fact abolitionist missions." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "chicanery" to describe the methods of the underground railroad, which are necessarily secretive and deceptive because the open pursuit of freedom is illegal.
ex_zh: The novel's chicanery (forged passes, hidden tunnels, coded messages) is the language of resistance—in a slave society, honesty is a luxury that the enslaved cannot afford.

def_en: a period of keeping awake during the night to watch or pray
def_zh: keep vigil, night vigil, candlelight vigil
quote_en: The family kept a vigil at their son's bedside while he remained in a coma.
quote_zh: "Eventually the body trade grew so reckless that relatives took to holding graveside vigils, lest their loved ones disappear in the night." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "vigil" to show how the slave system violates even the sanctity of death—relatives must guard the graves of their loved ones to prevent them from being stolen and dissected.
ex_zh: The novel's vigils (at gravesides, in attics, on the underground railroad) are all acts of desperate watchfulness, the constant alertness of those who can never rest.

def_en: having a skin disease that causes hair loss; shabby or squalid
def_zh: mangy dog, mangy coat, mangy appearance
quote_en: The mangy stray dog limped down the alley, looking for scraps.
quote_zh: "Carpenter snarled when he said the word, a mangy dog hoarding his bone: nigger." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "mangy" to describe Carpenter, whose own degraded state mirrors the degradation he inflicts on the bodies he steals—the grave robber is a mangy creature, but he is also a product of the same system.
ex_zh: The novel's mangy characters (Carpenter, the poor whites, the louts) are all at the bottom of the white social hierarchy, but they are still above the black population.

def_en: to burn slowly with smoke but no flame
def_zh: smoulder with anger, smoulder in the ashes, smoulder and burn
quote_en: The campfire smouldered for hours after the campers left.
quote_zh: "She had no idea how long the fire had been smouldering before it caught, but by the time the family roused from their sleep, the house was already lost." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "smoulder" to describe the hidden danger of the underground railroad—the fire of resistance smoulders beneath the surface, and when it finally catches, it destroys everything in its path.
ex_zh: The novel's smouldering fires (the burning house, the wildfire in Tennessee, the anger in Cora's heart) all represent the slow accumulation of heat that eventually explodes into violence.

def_en: the burial of a dead body
def_zh: proper interment, interment ceremony, interment site
quote_en: The soldier's interment at Arlington National Cemetery was attended by hundreds of mourners.
quote_zh: "Cora crawled back up the steps, perching just beneath the door, and listened—it might be day or night in the world above, and her interment in this underground station might last hours or weeks." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "interment" to describe Cora's time in the abandoned station, where she is buried alive—a living interment that mirrors the death of those who never made it out.
ex_zh: The novel's interments (of the dead, of the living in tunnels and attics, of the memory of the enslaved) all involve being placed underground and forgotten, but the underground railroad transforms interment into passage.

def_en: rough and harsh-sounding
def_zh: raspy voice, raspy cough, raspy whisper
quote_en: The old man's raspy voice cracked as he tried to sing the national anthem.
quote_zh: "She almost keeled over into the tracks as she howled after the train, her throat raspy and raw after days of privation." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "raspy" to describe Cora's voice after she has been abandoned in the tunnel—her raspy howl is the sound of a person pushed beyond endurance, a sound that comes from a place deeper than language.
ex_zh: The novel's raspy voices (Cora's, the engineer's, the dying) are all sounds of bodies pushed to their limits, a reminder that the underground railroad is a desperate struggle.

def_en: a state of lacking basic necessities; hardship
def_zh: suffer privation, economic privation, wartime privation
quote_en: The prisoners endured months of privation, with little food and no heat.
quote_zh: "She had starved before, when Connelly got it in his mind to punish the quarter and cut off rations, but the cotton demanded the punishment be brief—here, there was no way to know when she would eat next, a privation without end or purpose." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "privation" to describe the condition of the fugitive, who is always hungry, always cold, always at the edge of death—privation is not an interruption of life but the texture of it.
ex_zh: The novel's privations (hunger, cold, exhaustion) are the daily reality of the enslaved, and the fugitive's flight does not end privation but only changes its shape.

def_en: to delay or linger; to stay longer than intended
def_zh: tarry too long, tarry at the inn, tarry and wait
quote_en: We cannot tarry any longer if we want to catch the last train.
