quote_zh: "May the next traveler not tarry and keep moving up the line, all the way to liberty—Cora's prayer for whoever might next find themselves in the darkness." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "tarry" to warn against the temptation to linger in any station, any refuge, any moment of safety—to tarry is to risk capture, and the fugitive must always be moving.
ex_zh: The novel's warnings against tarrying (Cora's decision to stay in South Carolina, the Valentine farm's debate, the temptation to settle) suggest that in a slave society, no place is safe for long.

def_en: tender, juicy, and tasty
def_zh: succulent meat, succulent fruit, succulent and flavorful
quote_en: The roasted chicken was succulent and flavorful, cooked to perfection.
quote_zh: "The breeze tickled her skin and she gulped the air like water, the night sky the best meal she had ever had, the stars made succulent and ripe after her time below." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "succulent" to describe the taste of freedom, which is not a flavor but an experience—the air itself becomes succulent when you have been deprived of it for so long.
ex_zh: The novel's succulent moments (Cora emerging from the tunnel, the taste of clean water, the first breath of fresh air) are all sensations of release, capturing the physical pleasure of being alive after being buried in darkness.

def_en: to reveal or show the presence of a quality or feeling
def_zh: evince interest, evince concern, evince emotion
quote_en: The painting evinces a deep understanding of human suffering.
quote_zh: "For an agent of the underground railroad, presumably no stranger to peril and risk, Martin evinced a nervous personality, his round red face poking through the rubble as if he were the one who needed rescuing." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "evince" to describe Martin's demeanor, which reveals that the agents of the underground railroad are not superheroes but ordinary, fearful people.
ex_zh: The novel's evincing of fear (in Martin, in Sam, in Cora) shows that bravery is not the absence of fear but the willingness to act despite it.

def_en: moderate in eating and drinking; temperate
def_zh: abstemious diet, abstemious lifestyle, abstemious habits
quote_en: The monk led an abstemious life, eating only one small meal per day.
quote_zh: "Judge Tennyson was a respected figure in town when abstemious, but this night he tottered—the man who would sentence Martin and Ethel to the hanging tree could barely stand." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "abstemious" to describe the judge's sober condition, which is the only time he is respected—when he drinks, his moral corruption becomes visible.
ex_zh: The novel's abstemious figures (the judge when sober, the temperance advocates, the respectable citizens) are not necessarily more moral than the drunkards—their abstemiousness is a mask.

def_en: a false, absurd, or distorted representation of something
def_zh: travesty of justice, travesty of truth, travesty and mockery
quote_en: The trial was a travesty of justice, with the verdict decided before it began.
quote_zh: "She'd heard of coon shows but had never witnessed their travesties; the white men in burned cork capered through skits that mocked the very humanity of the people they pretended to portray." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "travesty" to describe the coon shows, which are a travesty not only of black people but of the very idea of representation—they show how the slave system turns human beings into caricatures.
ex_zh: The novel's travesties (the coon shows, the museum exhibits, the Friday night lynchings) are all performances that distort the truth of slavery for an audience that wants entertainment rather than confrontation.

def_en: difficult to control or predict because of willful or perverse behavior
def_zh: wayward child, wayward tendencies, wayward path
quote_en: The wayward teenager refused to follow any of the house rules.
quote_zh: "The wayward path of the fugitives through swamps and forests mirrored the chaos of a nation that could not decide whether to embrace freedom or tighten the chains of bondage." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "wayward" to describe both the fugitives' route and the nation's moral trajectory—neither follows a straight line, and both are marked by false turns and unexpected detours.
ex_zh: The novel's waywardness (Cora's journey, the underground railroad's routes, the plot itself) suggests that there is no clear path to freedom, only a wayward groping toward the light.

def_en: lying face down on the ground in submission or exhaustion; to weaken or overcome
def_zh: prostrate before the king, prostrate with grief, prostrate body
quote_en: The heat wave left the workers prostrate on the factory floor.
quote_zh: "The man who would sentence Martin and Ethel to the hanging tree could barely stand, prostrate from his own excesses." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "prostrate" to describe the judge's physical state, but also the moral prostration of a society that allows lynchings to be public entertainment.
ex_zh: The novel's prostrate bodies (Cora after the whippings, the slaves in the fields, the victims of the lynchings) are all evidence of the violence that keeps the system running.

def_en: the occasion of a person's birth; the place of origin
def_zh: nativity scene, nativity story, place of nativity
quote_en: The museum displayed a painting of the nativity scene from the 15th century.
quote_zh: "The nativity of the American nation was stained by the contradiction between its declaration of liberty and its dependence on slavery—a wound that would fester for centuries." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "nativity" to refer to the founding of America, which is a birth stained by original sin—the nation's nativity is not a moment of pure promise but a moment of hypocrisy.
ex_zh: The novel's treatment of nativity (Ajarry's origins in Africa, Cora's birth on the plantation, the nation's founding) suggests that where you come from determines where you can go.

def_en: not able to be maintained or defended against attack
def_zh: untenable position, untenable argument, untenable situation
quote_en: After the scandal, his position as director became untenable.
quote_zh: "The moral position of the slaveholding class was untenable, yet they clung to it with violence and terror, lynching any who dared to point out the obvious hypocrisy." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "untenable" to describe the slaveholders' ideology, which is logically and morally untenable but is maintained through force—the untenable becomes tenable when backed by violence.
ex_zh: The novel's untenable situations (the slave's position, the fugitive's hiding place, the underground railroad's stations) are all made untenable by the slave system, but the system itself is the most untenable of all.

def_en: a person who deserts one party or cause to join an opposing one
def_zh: turncoat traitor, political turncoat, turncoat and betrayer
quote_en: The general was branded a turncoat after switching sides during the war.
quote_zh: "On the plantation, a turncoat slave who informed on fellow fugitives could buy temporary safety, but the masters never truly trusted those who had betrayed their own kind." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "turncoat" to describe informers, whose betrayal is both understandable (they are trying to survive) and unforgivable (they condemn others to death).
ex_zh: The novel's turncoats (the slaves who inform, the white sympathizers who betray, the politicians who switch sides) all face the same dilemma: in a slave society, loyalty is impossible.

def_en: to reject or void, especially by legal procedure; to suppress forcefully
def_zh: quash a rebellion, quash a rumor, quash a subpoena
quote_en: The court moved to quash the subpoena, citing lack of evidence.
quote_zh: "The white militias were called upon to quash every whisper of rebellion, but the more violently they suppressed, the deeper the desire for freedom grew." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "quash" to describe the state's response to resistance—every attempt to quash rebellion only plants the seeds of the next one, because the desire for freedom cannot be quashed by violence.
ex_zh: The novel's quashed rebellions (the slave revolts, the underground railroad, the Valentine farm) are all temporarily suppressed, but they are never truly quashed—the spirit of resistance survives.

def_en: inciting or causing people to rebel against lawful authority
def_zh: seditious speech, seditious literature, seditious conspiracy
quote_en: The government banned the pamphlet as seditious material.
quote_zh: "Teaching a slave to read was considered a seditious act in most southern states, for literacy was the key that unlocked not only letters but the very idea that bondage could be escaped." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "seditious" to describe acts that are criminalized precisely because they threaten the foundations of the slave system—literacy is seditious because it gives the enslaved the tools to imagine freedom.
ex_zh: The novel's seditious acts (reading, running, hiding) are all treated as crimes by the state, but the novel itself is seditious, because it asks readers to imagine a world without slavery.

def_en: the quality of behaving with prudence and restraint; the freedom to decide
def_zh: judicial discretion, professional discretion, discretion advised
quote_en: The manager used her discretion to approve the refund without paperwork.
quote_zh: "The punishment for aiding a fugitive was left to the discretion of local authorities in North Carolina, which in practice meant death—for discretion in the hands of the mob was merely another word for vengeance." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "discretion" to expose the lie of local control—when punishment is left to local discretion, it means that the most violent elements of the community can do whatever they want.
ex_zh: The novel's discretion (the judge's, the patrollers', the mob's) is always exercised against the colored population, and the word becomes a mask for arbitrary violence.

def_en: a long-standing rival or archenemy; an agent of retribution
def_zh: arch-nemesis, nemesis of justice, meet one's nemesis
quote_en: The detective finally confronted his nemesis in a dark alley.
quote_zh: "Ridgeway became Cora's nemesis not because he was the most brutal slave catcher, but because he made the hunt personal—her family's escape was a flaw in the American imperative that only her recapture could repair." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "nemesis" to describe the relationship between Ridgeway and Cora, which is not just that of hunter and prey but of two people whose fates are intertwined—each is the other's nemesis.
ex_zh: The novel's nemeses (Ridgeway and Mabel, Ridgeway and Cora, the slave system and the fugitive) are locked in a dance that neither can escape.

def_en: a cause of great trouble or suffering
def_zh: trials and tribulations, tribulation and hardship, endure tribulation
quote_en: The family endured many tribulations during the long winter without heat.
quote_zh: "The tribulations of the underground railroad were etched into every mile of track laid in darkness, every fugitive who died within sight of freedom, every station master who swung from a tree." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "tribulation" to name the suffering that is inseparable from the struggle for freedom—there is no escape from tribulation, only the choice of which tribulation to endure.
ex_zh: The novel's tribulations (the whippings, the lynchings, the medical experiments) are the price that the enslaved pay for the chance of freedom.

def_en: to drink; to absorb or assimilate ideas or knowledge
def_zh: imbibe alcohol, imbibe knowledge, imbibe the culture
quote_en: The philosopher imbibed the teachings of the ancient Greeks during his years abroad.
quote_zh: "Caesar had imbibed from Mrs. Garner not only letters and reading but the dangerous belief that freedom was his birthright—a belief that would cost him his life." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "imbibe" to describe the absorption of ideas that are forbidden in the slave system—imbibing the belief in freedom is like drinking poison, because it makes you unable to accept your bondage.
ex_zh: The novel's imbibing (of literacy, of abolitionist ideas, of hope) is always dangerous, because the slave system punishes those who have imbibed the wrong beliefs—but it is also the only path to liberation.

def_en: to jump or dance around excitedly
def_zh: cavort and dance, cavort with joy, cavort in the field
quote_en: The children cavorted in the sprinklers on the hot summer day.
quote_zh: "The slaves were forced to cavort for the amusement of the Randall brothers, their pretended joy a mask for the terror that tightened around their hearts with every forced smile." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "cavort" to expose the coerced performance of happiness that is demanded of the enslaved—their cavorting is not joy but a survival strategy, a way of hiding their true feelings.
ex_zh: The novel's cavorting (at Jockey's birthday, at the Friday night festivals, on the plantation) is always ambiguous—is the dancer happy or terrified? The two are inseparable in the slave's experience.

def_en: an annual calendar containing weather forecasts, astronomical data, and other information
def_zh: farmer's almanac, weather almanac, old almanac
quote_en: The farmer consulted his almanac to determine the best time for planting.
quote_zh: "Cora found solace in Donald's old almanacs during her months in the attic, for the tides and moon phases proceeded without regard for white men's laws—a natural order that slavery could never entirely corrupt." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "almanac" to symbolize the natural world, which operates according to its own laws and offers a refuge from the arbitrary violence of the slave system—the moon rises whether the master wills it or not.
ex_zh: The novel's almanacs (Donald's, the one Royal gives Cora, the tables of tides and stars) represent a different kind of knowledge, one that is not owned by the white man and cannot be taken away.

def_en: deep, thoughtful reflection; the act of chewing cud
def_zh: deep rumination, rumination on the past, philosophical rumination
quote_en: After hours of rumination, he finally arrived at a solution to the problem.
quote_zh: "Cora's rumination on her mother's abandonment yielded no comfort, only the bitter realization that freedom sometimes required the most unforgivable betrayals." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "rumination" to describe Cora's obsessive thinking about her mother, which is both a way of processing trauma and a trap that keeps her stuck in the past—rumination can be a form of captivity.
ex_zh: The novel's ruminations (Cora's, Mabel's, the reader's) are all attempts to make sense of suffering, but the novel suggests that some suffering cannot be understood, only endured.

def_en: painful or laborious effort; the pain of childbirth
def_zh: hard travail, travail and suffering, travail of labor
quote_en: The monument honored the travail of the workers who built the railroad.
quote_zh: "The travail of the enslaved was not only in the cotton fields but in the endless reproduction of bodies for the market—each birth a profit, each death a line item on a ledger." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "travail" to name both the labor of the enslaved and the pain of childbirth, linking the two forms of suffering that are the foundation of the slave system.
ex_zh: The novel's travail (the cotton picking, the breeding, the escape) is the raw material of the slave economy, and the word reminds us that the wealth of America was built on the pain of black bodies.

def_en: the time of year when the sun is at its furthest point from the equator
def_zh: summer solstice, winter solstice, solstice celebration
quote_en: The ancient monument aligned perfectly with the rising sun on the summer solstice.
quote_zh: "On the winter solstice, the darkest night of the year, the fugitives huddled in the attic, knowing that each hour of darkness brought them closer to either capture or the thin, uncertain light of dawn." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "solstice" to mark the turning points in the fugitives' journey—the solstice is a moment of extreme darkness or extreme light, and the fugitives are always at the edge, never in the middle.
ex_zh: The novel's solstices (the longest night in the attic, the brightest day in South Carolina) are moments of transition, but for the fugitive, every day is a solstice.

def_en: involvement in a wrongful act or crime
def_zh: complicity in crime, silent complicity, complicity and guilt
quote_en: The guard's complicity in the escape was discovered after the investigation.
quote_zh: "Ethel's complicity in harboring Cora was not born of abolitionist conviction but of a twisted longing to possess a savage of her own—a complicity that would end with her tied to the hanging tree." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "complicity" to describe the many forms of involvement in the slave system—from the masters to the patrollers to the ordinary citizens who do nothing, everyone is complicit.
ex_zh: The novel's complicity (Ethel's, the gawkers', the readers') forces us to ask: where do we stand? Are we complicit in the violence we read about?

def_en: shriveled or wrinkled with age
def_zh: wizened old man, wizened face, wizened and bent
quote_en: The wizened old woman had lived alone in the mountain cabin for fifty years.
quote_zh: "The wizened face of Jockey, the oldest slave on the Randall plantation, told a story that no history book could capture—a century of survival through whips, chains, and the daily negation of his humanity." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "wizened" to describe Jockey's appearance, which is a map of his suffering—every wrinkle is a scar, every line a year of survival.
ex_zh: The novel's wizened characters (Jockey, Old Abraham, the Hob women) are all survivors, and their wizened bodies are evidence that the slave system does not always win.

def_en: refusal to change one's views or to agree
def_zh: political intransigence, intransigence and inflexibility, stubborn intransigence
quote_en: The intransigence of both sides made the peace negotiations impossible.
quote_zh: "The intransigence of the slaveholding class, their refusal to countenance any alternative to bondage, made civil war inevitable—and ensured that the underground railroad would run on blood as much as coal." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "intransigence" to describe the slaveholders' unwillingness to compromise, which doomed the nation to war—their intransigence was both the cause of the war and the justification for the underground railroad's existence.
ex_zh: The novel's intransigence (the masters', the fugitives', the abolitionists') is a double-edged sword—it can be a vice when it prevents compromise, but it can also be a virtue when it refuses to accept the unacceptable.

def_en: the omission of words from a sentence; a set of dots indicating such an omission
def_zh: ellipsis points, ellipsis in grammar, use of ellipsis
quote_en: The author used ellipsis to suggest the character's unspoken thoughts.
quote_zh: "Mabel's escape ended not with a triumphant arrival in the North but with an ellipsis—a body swallowed by the swamp, a story that Cora would spend her whole life trying to complete." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "ellipsis" as a structural device: the novel leaves gaps, omissions, things unsaid—Mabel's fate is an ellipsis, and the reader must fill in the missing words with imagination and grief.
ex_zh: The novel's ellipses (Mabel's disappearance, Lovey's fate, the gaps between sections) are invitations to the reader to complete the story, but also reminders that some stories cannot be fully told.

def_en: fine, feathery, or faint
def_zh: wispy clouds, wispy hair, wispy trail
quote_en: Wispy clouds drifted across the pale blue sky.
quote_zh: "The wispy remains of the slave quarters, barely visible through the morning mist, were all that was left of a generation of human beings who had lived, loved, and died without ever being called by their true names." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "wispy" to describe the traces of the enslaved that remain after they are gone—their presence is wispy, barely visible, easily overlooked, but it is still there for those who know how to look.
ex_zh: The novel's wispy traces (the abandoned cabins, the overgrown graves, the faded memories) are all that is left of millions of lives, and the wispy quality of these traces is a reproach to a nation that has tried to erase them.

def_en: a cause of great suffering; a whip used for punishment
def_zh: scourge of war, scourge of disease, scourge and torment
quote_en: Poverty was the scourge of the industrial city, claiming thousands each winter.
quote_zh: "The scourge of slavery was not merely the lash on the back but the systematic erasure of memory, language, and kinship—a violence that continued long after the chains were removed." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "scourge" to name slavery itself, which is a scourge not only in the sense of a whip but in the sense of a plague—slavery is a disease that infects the entire society.
ex_zh: The novel's scourges (the whip, the plantation, the medical experiments) are all instruments of torture, but the greatest scourge is the loss of memory—the erasure of the enslaved.

def_en: a violation or breach of a law or rule
def_zh: minor infraction, traffic infraction, infraction of rules
quote_en: The player received a penalty for a minor infraction of the rules.
quote_zh: "On the plantation, the smallest infraction—looking a white man in the eye, learning a letter, hesitating for a moment too long—could result in a whipping that left the back permanently scarred." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "infraction" to show how the slave system criminalizes normal human behavior—every action of the enslaved is potentially an infraction, because the law is designed to find fault with anything they do.
ex_zh: The novel's infractions (reading, running, looking) are all acts that would be innocent in a free society, but in the slave system they are capital crimes.

def_en: a brief evocative scene or description
def_zh: short vignette, vignette of life, literary vignette
quote_en: The book opens with a vignette of the protagonist's childhood in rural France.
quote_zh: "Each chapter of The Underground Railroad is a vignette of a different state, each with its own method of racial control—from South Carolina's medical experiments to North Carolina's public lynchings." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "vignette" to describe the novel's structure, which is not a continuous narrative but a series of brief, intense scenes that together create a mosaic of American slavery.
ex_zh: The novel's vignettes (Georgia, South Carolina, North Carolina, Tennessee, Indiana) are like museum dioramas—each is complete in itself, but together they tell a larger story.

def_en: a plot or scheme, especially one that is devious
def_zh: political machinations, machinations of power, behind-the-scenes machinations
quote_en: The novel exposes the political machinations behind the corporate takeover.
quote_zh: "The machinations of the slave catchers, from bribed informants to elaborate traps, were ultimately powerless against the simple, unquenchable human desire to be free." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "machination" to describe the complex systems of control that the slaveholders devise, but the novel suggests that all their machinations cannot extinguish the human spirit.
ex_zh: The novel's machinations (the underground railroad's codes, Ridgeway's strategies, the medical experiments) are all attempts to control what cannot be controlled, and the failure of machination is the novel's central hope.

def_en: pale and giving the appearance of illness or exhaustion
def_zh: wan smile, wan complexion, wan and weary
quote_en: She gave a wan smile after three sleepless nights in the hospital.
quote_zh: "The faces of the Hob women were wan and hollow-eyed, their bodies broken not only by labor but by the knowledge that the plantation had stolen everything from them except the slow, grinding misery of survival." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "wan" to describe the appearance of those who have been broken by the system—their wanness is the physical evidence of the violence done to them, a violence that leaves the body but does not kill it.
ex_zh: The novel's wan characters (the Hob women, the victims of the medical experiments, the survivors of the lynchings) are all still alive, but their wanness is a kind of death-in-life.

def_en: a clumsy, stupid person
def_zh: big lummox, clumsy lummox, lummox and oaf
quote_en: The lummox tripped over his own feet and spilled coffee on the client.
quote_zh: "Fiona called the night rider a lummox when he failed to discover Cora in the attic, but the real foolishness belonged to those who believed they could build a nation on slavery without eventually being consumed by its cruelty." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "lummox" as a term of abuse that Fiona hurls at the patroller, but the novel turns the insult back on the entire slave system—the real lummox is the system itself, which is clumsy, stupid, and ultimately self-destructive.
ex_zh: The novel's lummoxes (the night riders, the patrollers, the masters) are all agents of a system that is fundamentally stupid—it cannot see that it is digging its own grave.

def_en: a person sent to promote religion in a foreign country
def_zh: Christian missionary, missionary work, missionary zeal
quote_en: The missionary spent thirty years living among the tribe he sought to convert.
quote_zh: "Ethel thought it would be spiritually fulfilling to serve the Lord in dark Africa, delivering savages to the light. She dreamed of the ship that would take her." (Ethel)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "missionary" to critique the colonial mindset that sees non-white peoples as savages in need of civilizing—Ethel's missionary fantasies are a form of racism.
ex_zh: The novel's missionaries (Ethel, the white reformers in South Carolina, the abolitionists with their own agendas) all try to save the colored population according to their own terms, but true liberation cannot be given by missionaries—it must be taken by the fugitives themselves.

def_en: deceitful; acting in bad faith
def_zh: duplicitous behavior, duplicitous politician, duplicitous scheme
quote_en: The duplicitous politician promised reform while taking bribes from lobbyists.
quote_zh: "The game never lasted long before they switched to husband and wife, practicing kisses and arguments in the cellar of Ethel's house. Given the color of their skins, there was never any doubt over their roles." (Ethel)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "duplicitous" to describe the performances required by the color line—Ethel plays missionary, then wife, but the roles are built on a duplicitous foundation of racial hierarchy.
ex_zh: The novel's duplicitous characters (Ethel, the white sympathizers, the slave catchers who pose as abolitionists) all show that in a slave society, authenticity is impossible, and everyone wears a mask.

def_en: a representative sent on a mission
def_zh: emissary of peace, royal emissary, emissary and envoy
quote_en: The king sent an emissary to negotiate the terms of peace.
quote_zh: "The woman in the snow was the emissary of a dispossessed tribe. He bent himself to their service." (Indiana)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "emissary" to describe how a single fugitive can change a community—the dying woman becomes an emissary who transforms Valentine farm into a station of the underground railroad.
ex_zh: The novel's emissaries (the fugitives themselves, the survivors of the massacre, the agents of the railroad) carry a message that the system wants to suppress, and the novel is itself an emissary.

def_en: to free someone from a mistaken belief
def_zh: disabuse of the notion, disabuse of ignorance, disabuse a misconception
quote_en: She tried to disabuse him of the notion that wealth would bring happiness.
quote_zh: "Her father explained the origin of the negro to disabuse her of this colorful idea. Some maintained that the negro was the remnant of a race of giants." (Ethel)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "disabuse" to show how racist ideology is taught to children—Ethel's father disabuses her of her innocent friendship with Jasmine, replacing it with the curse of Ham.
ex_zh: The novel's disabusing (of Ethel, of Caesar, of the reader) is a painful process—the novel disabuses us of any romantic notions about slavery, showing the whip, the chain, and the auction block.

def_en: influence or power, especially in politics or business
def_zh: political clout, economic clout, clout and influence
quote_en: The former senator still has considerable clout in Washington.
quote_zh: "Her mother held a low opinion about her husband going upstairs but was not without resources. When their family sold Jasmine to the coppersmith, Ethel knew it was her mother's doing." (Ethel)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "clout" to describe the limited power that white women could exercise within the slave system—Ethel's mother uses her clout to remove Jasmine, but she cannot dismantle the system itself.
ex_zh: The novel's clout (the masters', the slave catchers', the state's) is the central fact of the slave system—clout is the ability to do violence without consequence.

def_en: a storehouse for threshed grain
def_zh: grain granary, community granary, granary and silo
quote_en: The village granary was filled with enough wheat to last through the winter.
quote_zh: "The fertile lands of the American South became a granary for the industrial North, but the grain was watered by the sweat and blood of enslaved men, women, and children." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "granary" to expose the economic relationship between the slave South and the industrial North—the granary is built on the labor of the enslaved, and the North profits while pretending to be morally superior.
ex_zh: The novel's granaries (the cotton fields, the rice plantations, the corn fields) are all sites of extraction, where the labor of the enslaved is harvested and shipped north.

def_en: to stare in an angry or sullen way
def_zh: glower at, glower with anger, glower and scowl
quote_en: The teacher glowered at the student who had been talking during the lesson.
quote_zh: "Ridgeway's glower could silence a room, but Cora learned to meet his gaze without flinching—a small rebellion that reminded him that even in chains, a slave's spirit could not be entirely broken." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "glower" to describe Ridgeway's intimidating stare, which is a weapon of psychological control—but Cora's refusal to be cowed is an act of resistance.
ex_zh: The novel's glowers (Ridgeway's, Terrance's, the patrollers') are all attempts to dominate through the eyes, but the glower loses its power when the person being glowered at refuses to look away.

def_en: to strip of covering or possessions
def_zh: denude the land, denude of vegetation, denude of dignity
quote_en: The logging operation denuded the hillside of all vegetation.
quote_zh: "The white settlers denuded the land of its forests and its original inhabitants, clearing the way for cotton—a crop that would in turn denude the bodies of the enslaved who worked it." (Tennessee)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "denude" to describe the double violence of colonialism: the land is denuded of its forests and its people, and then the bodies of the enslaved are denuded of their dignity.
ex_zh: The novel's denuded landscapes (the burned forests of Tennessee, the cleared fields of Georgia) are the physical evidence of the violence that made America.

def_en: to inspire or permeate with a feeling or quality
def_zh: imbue with meaning, imbue with spirit, imbue with hope
quote_en: The mentor sought to imbue his students with a love of learning.
quote_zh: "The underground railroad imbued the word 'freedom' with a meaning far beyond the dictionary—it meant risking death, betraying family, and walking hundreds of miles through swamps." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "imbue" to describe how the underground railroad transforms abstract concepts into lived experience—freedom is imbued with sweat, blood, terror, and hope.
ex_zh: The novel's imbuing (of freedom, of hope, of the underground railroad) is the central act of the narrative—the novel imbues historical facts with the emotional weight of individual experience.

def_en: very attentive to detail; fussy
def_zh: fastidious about cleanliness, fastidious dresser, fastidious and meticulous
quote_en: The fastidious editor caught every typo in the manuscript.
quote_zh: "Homer was fastidious about his black suit and stovepipe hat, extracting lint from the fabric as if it were a poison spider—a strange precision in a boy who helped his master chain human beings for a living." (Tennessee)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "fastidious" to describe Homer's attention to his appearance, which is a form of dignity in a boy who has no other dignity—his fastidiousness is a way of asserting control over his own body.
ex_zh: The novel's fastidious characters (Homer, the doctors, the museum curator) show that fastidiousness can be either a form of resistance or a form of cruelty, depending on who is being fastidious and why.

def_en: to bully or harass
def_zh: hector and intimidate, hector the staff, hector into submission
quote_en: The older boys would hector the newcomers until they cried.
quote_zh: "The overseer hectored the field hands constantly, his whip always within reach, but the slaves learned that the true power of the oppressor lies not in the lash but in the victim's belief that resistance is impossible." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "hector" to describe the constant harassment that is the texture of plantation life—the hectoring is designed to break the spirit, but it only works if the victim accepts it.
ex_zh: The novel's hectoring (the overseers, the patrollers, the masters) is the daily language of the slave system, but the fugitives learn to resist hectoring by refusing to internalize it.

def_en: various; of different kinds
def_zh: sundry items, sundry expenses, sundry and diverse
quote_en: The general store sold sundry items from buttons to horse harnesses.
quote_zh: "The station master's duties included sundry tasks—hiding fugitives, forging passes, bribing officials, and digging graves for those who did not make it." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "sundry" to describe the miscellaneous, often overlooked tasks that keep the underground railroad running—the sundry duties are not glamorous, but they are essential.
ex_zh: The novel's sundry characters (the station agents, the cooks, the drivers) are the unsung heroes of the underground railroad, and the sundry tasks they perform are the small, daily acts of resistance that make liberation possible.

def_en: showing great attention to detail
def_zh: meticulous planning, meticulous attention, meticulous and thorough
quote_en: The jeweler's meticulous workmanship made each piece unique.
quote_zh: "The underground railroad required meticulous planning—safe houses spaced exactly one night's journey apart, signals that could be read only by the initiated, and codes buried in newspaper classifieds that could mean life or death." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "meticulous" to describe the planning that goes into every escape—the underground railroad is not a spontaneous uprising but a carefully organized network.
ex_zh: The novel's meticulous details (the coded messages, the timing of departures, the selection of safe houses) are the machinery of liberation, showing that freedom is not won by accident but by painstaking work.

def_en: to hit repeatedly; to defeat decisively
def_zh: clobber the opponent, clobber with a club, get clobbered
quote_en: The boxer clobbered his opponent in the first round.
quote_zh: "Boseman clobbered the Indian scout with a shovel to win his necklace of ears, but Ridgeway knew that brute force alone could not catch Mabel's daughter." (Tennessee)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "clobber" to describe the brute violence that is one tool of the slave catcher—clobbering is effective in the moment, but it is not enough to capture someone like Cora.
ex_zh: The novel's clobbering (Boseman's violence, the patrollers' beatings, the mob's lynchings) is the crude, obvious violence of the slave system, but the more insidious violence is the psychological kind.

def_en: having a feeling of vague or regretful longing
def_zh: wistful look, wistful sigh, wistful memory
quote_en: She gave a wistful sigh as she watched the children play outside.
quote_zh: "Boseman stared at the hills with a wistful expression, remembering his father who had passed through the Cherokee lands before the Trail of Tears—a nostalgia for a country that had already been stolen." (Tennessee)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "wistful" to describe Boseman's nostalgia, which is a form of mourning for a world that no longer exists—his wistfulness is not for the loss of the Cherokee but for his own past.
ex_zh: The novel's wistful moments (Boseman's, Ethel's, the white settlers') are all tinged with irony—the wistfulness is for things that were stolen from others.

def_en: enthusiastic or passionate
def_zh: ardent supporter, ardent desire, ardent love
quote_en: She was an ardent supporter of women's suffrage throughout her life.
quote_zh: "Lander was an ardent believer in the power of words to change the world, but his eloquence could not stop the bullets that cut him down on the Valentine farm." (Indiana)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "ardent" to describe Lander's passionate commitment to the cause, but the novel is ambivalent about ardor—ardor is inspiring, but it is also vulnerable.
ex_zh: The novel's ardent characters (Lander, Royal, the abolitionists) are all people who believe deeply in the possibility of change, but the novel shows that ardor without strategy is suicide.

def_en: persuasive language; the art of effective speaking
def_zh: political rhetoric, empty rhetoric, rhetoric of freedom
quote_en: The candidate's rhetoric won over the crowd, but his promises proved empty.
quote_zh: "The rhetoric of the Declaration of Independence—'all men are created equal'—was recited by slaves like Michael on the Randall plantation, each word a bitter mockery of the nation that refused to apply its own principles to them." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "rhetoric" to expose the gap between American ideals and American reality—the rhetoric of liberty is beautiful, but it is also a lie.
ex_zh: The novel's rhetoric (the Declaration, the speeches of Lander, the words of the masters) is always suspect—words can be used to liberate or to deceive.

def_en: a bruise; an injury that does not break the skin
def_zh: multiple contusions, contusion of the arm, contusion and abrasion
quote_en: The doctor noted multiple contusions on the patient's arms and torso.
quote_zh: "The contusions on Cora's body from Ridgeway's beatings were nothing compared to the internal scars left by watching Caesar dragged to the jail and Lovey disappear into the night." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "contusion" to name the visible injuries that the slave system inflicts, but the invisible injuries—the psychological wounds, the traumas, the losses—are more damaging and longer-lasting.
ex_zh: The novel's contusions (the whip marks, the scars, the bruises) are the physical evidence of violence, but the novel's true subject is the damage that cannot be seen.

def_en: great embarrassment or shame; the discipline of the body
def_zh: mortification and shame, public mortification, sense of mortification
quote_en: To his mortification, he realized he had been speaking with his fly unzipped.
quote_zh: "Ethel's mortification at being exposed as a harbor of fugitives was swallowed by the greater terror of the hanging tree—a reminder that shame is a luxury reserved for those who are not fighting for their lives." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "mortification" to describe Ethel's shame, but the novel suggests that mortification is a privilege of the powerful—the enslaved have no time for mortification because they are too busy trying to survive.
ex_zh: The novel's mortifications (Ethel's, the white townspeople's, the reader's) are all forms of discomfort that are minor compared to the suffering of the enslaved.

def_en: lacking in quantity or quality; sparse
def_zh: meagre portion, meagre income, meagre resources
quote_en: The refugees survived on a meagre ration of rice and beans each day.
quote_zh: "The meagre possessions that fugitives carried—a handful of yams, a flint, a stolen pair of shoes—were worth more than gold, for they represented the difference between reaching the North and dying in the swamp." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "meagre" to describe the fugitives' supplies, but the novel shows that meagre resources can be enough if they are used wisely—the yams are meagre, but they keep Cora alive.
ex_zh: The novel's meagre possessions (the yams, the flint, the stolen shoes) are the material basis of the fugitive's survival, and the novel insists that we value the meagre because it is all the fugitive has.

def_en: an adventurous or reckless incident
def_zh: youthful escapade, daring escapade, escapade and adventure
quote_en: The boys' weekend escapade involved camping in a forbidden forest.
quote_zh: "Mabel's overnight escapade into the swamp was meant to be a brief taste of freedom before returning to her daughter, but a cottonmouth's bite transformed a small adventure into a death sentence." (Mabel)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "escapade" to describe Mabel's flight, but the word is ironic—what seems like an escapade is actually a matter of life and death.
ex_zh: The novel's escapades (Mabel's, Cora's, the underground railroad's) are all framed as adventures, but the novel strips away the romance to show the terror—an escapade is only an escapade if you survive.

def_en: difficult to understand or interpret
def_zh: inscrutable expression, inscrutable mystery, inscrutable and opaque
quote_en: The detective faced an inscrutable smile from the suspect.
quote_zh: "Homer's inscrutable expression, whether he was chaining a fugitive or recording Ridgeway's dictation, made him seem like a creature from another world—a black boy who had made himself indispensable to the man who hunted his own kind." (Tennessee)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "inscrutable" to describe Homer, whose motives and emotions are impossible to read—his inscrutability is a defense mechanism, a way of surviving in a world where showing your feelings can get you killed.
ex_zh: The novel's inscrutable characters (Homer, the Hob women, the survivors) all wear masks that are impossible to penetrate, and the novel suggests that inscrutability is not a character trait but a survival strategy.

def_en: to work with or use diligently; to travel a route regularly
def_zh: ply one's trade, ply the waters, ply between
quote_en: The boat plies the river between the two cities every morning.
quote_zh: "The slave catcher's wagon plied the roads of the South, each mile a fresh opportunity for cruelty, but the underground railroad's tracks plied a different geography—one that existed in darkness." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "ply" to contrast two different geographies—the open roads of the slave catcher and the hidden tunnels of the underground railroad—both are plied regularly, but one is visible and one is secret.
ex_zh: The novel's plying (of the underground railroad, of the slave catcher's routes, of the fugitive's path) maps a territory that exists nowhere but in the imagination of the fugitive and the reader.

def_en: a structure for hanging criminals
def_zh: gallows and gibbet, gallows humor, sent to the gallows
quote_en: The condemned man was led to the gallows at dawn.
quote_zh: "The gallows on the Randall plantation stood as a permanent warning, but for every slave who saw them and stayed, another looked at the same wood and decided that death by hanging was preferable to one more day in chains." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "gallows" as a symbol of the slave system's ultimate threat—the gallows are meant to terrify, but the novel shows that terror can backfire, turning the threat of death into a promise of release.
ex_zh: The novel's gallows (the hanging tree in North Carolina, the gallows on the Randall plantation, the bodies on the Freedom Trail) are all meant to enforce submission, but they also create martyrs—the gallows cannot kill the idea of freedom.

def_en: charming or enchanting, often deceptively
def_zh: beguiling smile, beguiling promise, beguiling and seductive
quote_en: The beguiling advertisement promised wealth but delivered only debt.
quote_zh: "South Carolina's beguiling promise of colored uplift—good wages, education, housing—was a mask for the sterilization programs and medical experiments that treated black bodies as raw material for white science." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "beguiling" to describe the promises of South Carolina, which are designed to seduce the colored population into compliance—the beguiling surface hides a terrible reality.
ex_zh: The novel's beguiling surfaces (the whitewashed dormitories, the Griffin Building, the museum exhibits) are all attractive but deceptive—the novel teaches us to look beneath the beguiling surface to see the violence hidden behind the beauty.

def_en: the process of declining or worsening
def_zh: moral degeneration, physical degeneration, degeneration and decay
quote_en: The degeneration of the old building was accelerated by years of neglect.
quote_zh: "Ridgeway saw Terrance Randall's degeneration from competent master to drunken sadist as a cautionary tale: the plantation system did not just corrupt the enslaved; it also rotted the souls of those who profited from it." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "degeneration" to describe the effect of slavery on the masters—the system does not only degrade the enslaved; it also degrades the enslavers, turning them into monsters.
ex_zh: The novel's degenerations (Terrance's, the slave catchers', the entire slaveholding class) are the moral costs of the system—no one emerges from slavery unscathed.

def_en: a woman who lives with a man but is not his wife, especially in societies where such arrangements are recognized
def_zh: royal concubine, concubine and consort, kept as a concubine
quote_en: The emperor kept several concubines in the forbidden palace.
quote_zh: "Terrance Randall treated the enslaved women of his plantation as concubines to be taken at will, his power to violate without consequence the ultimate expression of the dehumanization at slavery's core." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "concubine" to name the sexual exploitation that is central to the slave system—the enslaved woman's body is not her own, and the master's right to use it as he wishes is a fundamental feature of slavery.
ex_zh: The novel's concubines (the women taken by Terrance, the women in the dormitories, the victims of the patrollers) are all denied the dignity of consent, and the word exposes the sexual violence often hidden behind euphemisms.

def_en: a young dog; an insolent youth
def_zh: wolf whelp, whelp and cub, insolent whelp
quote_en: The mother dog nursed her whelps in the warm corner of the barn.
quote_zh: "Ridgeway dismissed Terrance as a whelp who had inherited his father's plantation but none of his competence—yet even a whelp with a whip was more than dangerous enough to destroy the lives of every slave under his control." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "whelp" as a term of contempt for Terrance, but the novel shows that even a contemptible whelp can do enormous damage when given absolute power.
ex_zh: The novel's whelps (Terrance, the young patrollers, the children of masters) are all products of the slave system, and their whelpishness does not excuse their cruelty—the novel insists that we hold them accountable.

def_en: a natural object or animal believed to have spiritual significance
def_zh: totem pole, animal totem, totem and symbol
quote_en: The tribe's totem was a wolf, representing loyalty and courage.
quote_zh: "The burned oak tree in the park of the North Carolina town became a totem of terror, its branches decorated not with sacred symbols but with the bodies of those who had dared to believe that freedom was possible." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "totem" to describe the hanging tree, which has become a sacred object for the white community—it is a totem of white supremacy, a symbol of the violence that maintains the racial order.
ex_zh: The novel's totems (the hanging tree, the whipping post, the auction block) are all objects of veneration for the slave system—they are the sacred symbols of a religion of violence.

def_en: affected by yellowing of the skin; cynical or prejudiced
def_zh: jaundiced view, jaundiced eye, jaundiced perspective
quote_en: The journalist had a jaundiced view of politics after decades of covering corruption.
quote_zh: "Ridgeway's jaundiced eye saw every act of kindness as weakness and every slave's hope as a flaw to be corrected, but his cynicism could not extinguish the one thing he most despised: the stubborn, irrational, undying belief that freedom was worth dying for." (Tennessee)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "jaundiced" to describe Ridgeway's worldview, which is colored by his hatred—his jaundiced eye sees only what it wants to see, and it cannot see the hope that drives the fugitives.
ex_zh: The novel's jaundiced characters (Ridgeway, Terrance, the slave catchers) are all blind to the humanity of the enslaved, and their jaundiced vision is a form of moral disease.

def_en: a rude or ill-mannered person
def_zh: boor and lout, ignorant boor, drunken boor
quote_en: The boor interrupted every conversation with loud, ignorant comments.
quote_zh: "The mayor was a corrupt boor, Ridgeway observed, but even a boor could recognize profit—and the slave catcher's business depended on the greed of men who would sell their own mothers for a few extra bales of cotton." (Tennessee)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "boor" to describe the corrupt officials who enable the slave system—they are boors, but their boorishness does not prevent them from being effective agents of oppression.
ex_zh: The novel's boors (the mayor, the judge, the patrollers) are all coarse, uneducated men, but they are also powerful—the novel shows that boorishness is not an obstacle to power but often its companion.

def_en: childishly sulky or bad-tempered
def_zh: petulant child, petulant remark, petulant and whining
quote_en: The petulant child threw his toys when he didn't get his way.
quote_zh: "Terrance's petulant cruelty—whipping a slave for a drop of wine on his cuff—revealed that his power was not strength but a child's tantrum armed with a cane, capable of destroying lives on a whim." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "petulant" to describe Terrance's cruelty, which is not the calculated cruelty of a strategic oppressor but the random cruelty of a spoiled child—his petulance makes him more dangerous because he is unpredictable.
ex_zh: The novel's petulant characters (Terrance, the young masters, the capricious slaveholders) show that the slave system is not a rational system but an arbitrary one—petulance is a feature, not a bug.

def_en: a period of isolation to prevent the spread of disease
def_zh: quarantine zone, quarantine period, quarantine and isolation
quote_en: The ship was placed under quarantine after several passengers showed signs of fever.
quote_zh: "The town of yellow fever became a quarantine zone that even Ridgeway dared not enter, but the moral quarantine of the South—its isolation from the growing antislavery sentiment of the North—could not stop the underground railroad." (Tennessee)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "quarantine" to describe both the literal isolation of a disease-stricken town and the metaphorical isolation of the South from abolitionist ideas—the underground railroad is the disease that breaks the quarantine.
ex_zh: The novel's quarantines (the fever town, the slave states, the plantation) are all attempts to contain what cannot be contained—the desire for freedom is contagious, and no quarantine can stop it.

def_en: an act that violates a law or command
def_zh: moral transgression, transgression of boundaries, transgression and sin
quote_en: His transgression of the company's ethics policy resulted in immediate termination.
quote_zh: "For a slave, the only true transgression was being born black in a white man's country; every attempt to escape, to learn, to love, to live was punished as a crime against the natural order of things." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "transgression" to name the supposed crime of being black in America—the system defines black existence itself as a transgression, and the enslaved are punished not for what they do but for who they are.
ex_zh: The novel's transgressions (reading, running, loving) are all acts that would be innocent in a free society, but in the slave system they are capital crimes—the novel shows that the concept of transgression is a tool of oppression.

def_en: a punishment or fate that someone deserves
def_zh: get one's comeuppance, final comeuppance, deserved comeuppance
quote_en: The arrogant CEO finally got his comeuppance when the company collapsed.
quote_zh: "Ridgeway believed that runaways deserved their comeuppance, but in the end, it was he who lay broken at the bottom of a tunnel, whispering his American imperative to a black boy who wrote it all down—a comeuppance delivered by the very people he had hunted." (The North)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "comeuppance" to describe Ridgeway's fate, which is a form of poetic justice—the slave catcher becomes the victim, and his comeuppance is administered by the fugitive he could not catch.
ex_zh: The novel's comeuppances (Ridgeway's, Terrance's, the slave system's) are all partial and incomplete—the novel does not offer easy justice, but it does offer a glimpse of a world where the hunters become the hunted.

def_en: based on random choice or personal whim rather than reason
def_zh: arbitrary decision, arbitrary rules, arbitrary and capricious
quote_en: The teacher's grading seemed arbitrary, with no clear criteria.
quote_zh: "Terrance Randall's arbitrary punishments—a beating for a smile, a day in the stocks for a sneeze—were designed not to correct behavior but to remind every slave that their lives depended on the unpredictable mercy of a man who had none." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "arbitrary" to describe the cruelty of the slave system, which is not governed by reason or justice but by the whims of the masters—the arbitrariness is the point, because it keeps the enslaved constantly off-balance.
ex_zh: The novel's arbitrary violence (Terrance's, the patrollers', the mob's) is the signature of the slave system—the system is not rational, it is arbitrary, and the arbitrariness is a form of terror.

def_en: refusing to be persuaded or changed
def_zh: adamant refusal, adamant stance, adamant and unyielding
quote_en: Despite the evidence, she remained adamant that her conclusion was correct.
quote_zh: "Caesar was adamant that Cora must join his escape, and his certainty—unshakeable even when she refused—eventually convinced her to risk everything, proving that sometimes the most stubborn fool is also the one who sees the truth most clearly." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "adamant" to describe Caesar's determination, which is a form of stubbornness—his adamant refusal to take no for an answer is what saves Cora.
ex_zh: The novel's adamant characters (Caesar, Cora, the underground railroad agents) are all people who refuse to give up, even when the odds are against them—their adamant persistence is the engine of the narrative.

def_en: simple and unsophisticated; made at home
def_zh: homespun wisdom, homespun fabric, homespun philosophy
quote_en: The old man offered homespun wisdom about the value of hard work.
quote_zh: "The homespun clothes of the slaves—rough negro cloth that chafed the skin—were a uniform of bondage, but the same hands that wore them also wove the coded quilts that guided fugitives north on the underground railroad." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "homespun" to describe the material culture of the enslaved—their clothes are homespun, but so are their codes, their quilts, their songs—the homespun is the medium of resistance.
ex_zh: The novel's homespun objects (the quilts, the songs, the stories) are all forms of folk knowledge that the masters overlook because they are homespun—the novel insists that we pay attention to the homespun, because the homespun is where the resistance lives.

def_en: a short, simple song
def_zh: sing a ditty, folk ditty, cheerful ditty
quote_en: The children sang a cheerful ditty as they walked to school.
quote_zh: "Jasper's monotonous ditties drove Ridgeway to murder, but the hymns of the enslaved were not merely noise—they were coded maps, calls to resistance, and laments for the dead that the white men would never understand." (Tennessee)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "ditty" to describe the songs of the enslaved, which sound simple to white ears but carry complex meanings—the ditty is a form of code, a way of communicating in plain sight.
ex_zh: The novel's ditties (Jasper's hymns, the work songs, the spirituals) are all examples of the oral tradition that sustained the enslaved—the ditty is not just entertainment but a form of resistance and a means of organizing rebellion.

def_en: to bring under control or domination
def_zh: subjugate the population, subjugate and oppress, subjugate by force
quote_en: The empire sought to subjugate all neighboring territories.
quote_zh: "The white men of North Carolina sought to subjugate not only the black population but the very idea of black existence, passing laws that erased the race entirely—a subjugation so complete that even the memory of the enslaved was to be extinguished." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "subjugate" to describe the project of North Carolina's race laws, which aim not just to control the black population but to erase them from history—subjugation is not enough; the state wants annihilation.
ex_zh: The novel's subjugations (of the enslaved, of the Indians, of the poor whites) are all part of the same American project—the subjugation of anyone who is not a white landowner.

def_en: a short period of rest or relief
def_zh: brief respite, welcome respite, respite from suffering
quote_en: The cool breeze offered a brief respite from the summer heat.
quote_zh: "Jockey's birthday provided the slaves of Randall plantation with a rare respite from the daily grind of the cotton rows, but even as they danced, the shadow of the overseer's whip waited at the edge of the circle." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "respite" to describe the brief moments of relief that the enslaved are allowed—the respite is always temporary, always conditional, always shadowed by the threat of violence.
ex_zh: The novel's respites (Jockey's birthday, the Saturday nights on Valentine farm, the moments between whippings) are all fragile—they are not safe havens but pauses in the violence.

def_en: rude or disrespectful behavior
def_zh: insolence and impertinence, act of insolence, intolerable insolence
quote_en: The manager would not tolerate any insolence from the new employees.
quote_zh: "For a slave, insolence could be a look held too long, a question asked, a foot that failed to step aside quickly enough—each act of perceived insolence a capital crime in a system that demanded absolute submission." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "insolence" to name the supposed crime of asserting one's humanity in the presence of a master—the system defines any assertion of self as insolence, and the punishment for insolence is death.
ex_zh: The novel's insolence (Cora's look, Caesar's question, the fugitive's flight) are all acts that the system calls insolent, but the novel redefines insolence as courage—the insolent slave is the one who refuses to submit.

def_en: the realization or fulfillment of a plan or project
def_zh: come to fruition, reach fruition, fruition of dreams
quote_en: After years of planning, her dream of opening a bakery came to fruition.
quote_zh: "Caesar's plan for escape came to fruition not in the way he imagined—with both of them reaching freedom—but in Cora's survival, which became the fruition of every hope he had carried from Virginia to Georgia." (Caesar)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "fruition" to describe the outcome of Caesar's plan, which is not the happy ending he hoped for but a partial fulfillment—his hope bears fruit, but the fruit is bitter.
ex_zh: The novel's fruitions (Caesar's plan, Cora's escape, the underground railroad's success) are all partial, incomplete, and tragic—the novel refuses easy happy endings, but it also refuses despair, because even partial fruition is better than none.

def_en: concise and exact use of words; shortness of time
def_zh: brevity and conciseness, brevity of life, for brevity's sake
quote_en: The brevity of his speech was appreciated by the tired audience.
quote_zh: "The brevity of a slave's life on the harshest plantations was measured in years, but the brevity of a free moment—a dance at Jockey's birthday, a stolen kiss in the schoolhouse—could sustain a soul through decades of suffering." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "brevity" to contrast the shortness of life with the shortness of joy—both are brief, but the brevity of joy is precious because it is all the enslaved have.
ex_zh: The novel's brevities (the dances, the kisses, the moments of respite) are all fleeting, but the novel insists that brevity is not a reason to despair—the brevity of joy makes it more intense, and a brief taste of freedom can fuel a lifetime of hope.

def_en: sly or cunning intelligence
def_zh: guile and deceit, use guile, guile and trickery
quote_en: The fox used guile to escape the trap set by the hunter.
quote_zh: "Guile and pluck, guile and pluck. The white man in the book, Gulliver, roved from peril to peril... That was white people all over." (Caesar)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "guile" to describe Cora's survival strategy, which is not about brute strength but about cunning, patience, and intelligence—guile is the weapon of the weak.
ex_zh: The novel's guile (Cora's, the underground railroad's, the fugitives') is the central theme of the narrative—the novel itself is an act of guile, using the tools of the oppressor to tell the story of the oppressed.

def_en: a person who intrudes or meddles where they are not welcome
def_zh: unwelcome interloper, interloper and intruder, interloper in the family
quote_en: The old dog growled at the interloper who dared to enter his yard.
quote_zh: "Cora felt like an interloper in the schoolhouse, surrounded by children who had learned their letters while she had been dodging the overseer's whip." (Indiana)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "interloper" to describe Cora's feeling of not belonging, which is imposed by the slave system—the system makes her feel like an interloper in spaces that should be open to her.
ex_zh: The novel's interlopers (Cora, the fugitives, the black students in white schools) are all people who are made to feel unwelcome where they have every right to be—the interloper is not the problem; the problem is the system that defines them as interlopers.

def_en: causing annoyance or frustration
def_zh: vexing problem, vexing question, vexing and irritating
quote_en: The vexing problem took the entire team three days to solve.
quote_zh: "The most vexing question for the fugitives was not how to escape the plantation but how to trust anyone again after so many betrayals." (Indiana)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "vexing" to describe the question of trust, which is a vexing problem for the fugitives—they cannot survive without help, but they cannot trust without risking betrayal.
ex_zh: The novel's vexing questions (whom to trust, where to go, when to run) have no easy answers—the vexing is the texture of the fugitive's life, and the novel does not resolve the vexing but instead dwells in it.

def_en: to harass or pester persistently
def_zh: badger with questions, badger and nag, badger into action
quote_en: The reporters continued to badger the politician with uncomfortable questions.
quote_zh: "Sybil would badger Cora into joining the quilting circle, insisting that the girl needed more than just survival—she needed something to make her feel human again." (Indiana)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "badger" to describe Sybil's persistence in getting Cora to participate in community life—the badgering is a form of love, a way of pulling Cora out of her isolation.
ex_zh: The novel's badgering (Sybil's, the Hob women's, the underground railroad agents') is all about refusing to let the enslaved disappear into their own trauma—the badgering is an insistence on community.

def_en: a person who acts as a link between parties
def_zh: financial intermediary, intermediary in negotiations, act as intermediary
quote_en: The diplomat served as an intermediary between the warring nations.
quote_zh: "The underground railroad relied on a network of intermediaries—station agents, conductors, sympathizers—each one a fragile link in a chain that stretched from Georgia to Canada." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "intermediary" to name the essential role of those who connect fugitives to the next stage of their journey—the intermediaries are the hidden infrastructure of liberation.
ex_zh: The novel's intermediaries (Fletcher, Sam, Lumbly, Royal) are all ordinary people who take extraordinary risks—they are the glue that holds the underground railroad together.

def_en: a person who makes or sells women's hats
def_zh: milliner's shop, milliner and dressmaker, milliner's craft
quote_en: The milliner displayed her finest bonnets in the shop window.
quote_zh: "After escaping to the North, Sybil worked for a milliner in Worcester, stitching ribbons onto hats for white ladies who would never have spoken to her on the street." (Indiana)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "milliner" to describe the kind of work that fugitive women did in the North—respectable, low-paid, invisible labor that kept them alive but did not lift them out of poverty.
ex_zh: The novel's milliners (Sybil, the women of the Valentine farm, the domestic workers) are all engaged in the work of survival—the milliner's shop is not a paradise, but it is not the plantation either.

def_en: abstinence from alcoholic drink; moderation
def_zh: temperance movement, temperance society, temperance and sobriety
quote_en: The temperance movement gained momentum in the nineteenth century.
quote_zh: "On the Valentine farm, temperance was the rule except on Saturday nights, when those with a taste for spirits partook and had something to think about at the next morning's sermon." (Indiana)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "temperance" to describe the rule of the Valentine farm, which is not a prohibition but a moderation—the farm allows for celebration, but it also insists on discipline.
ex_zh: The novel's temperance (the Valentine farm's rules, the fugitives' self-restraint) is all about the rejection of the excesses of the slave system—the masters indulge in cruelty, while the fugitives practice temperance.

def_en: a stand for holding a book or notes while speaking
def_zh: podium and lectern, stand at the lectern, lectern and microphone
quote_en: The professor adjusted the microphone on the lectern before beginning his lecture.
quote_zh: "Lander gripped the lectern as he spoke, his words weaving a spell over the audience, but the wooden stand could not protect him from the bullets that would soon tear through the meeting house." (Indiana)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "lectern" to symbolize the power of oratory, but also its vulnerability—the lectern is a place of authority, but it is also a target.
ex_zh: The novel's lecterns (Lander's, the abolitionists', the preachers') are all sites of resistance and danger—the lectern is where the truth is spoken, and the slave system cannot tolerate the truth.

def_en: having a friendly, informal manner
def_zh: folksy charm, folksy humor, folksy and down-home
quote_en: The candidate's folksy charm appealed to rural voters.
quote_zh: "Gloria worked hard on eliminating her plantation inflections, but when conversation took a folksy turn, she would slip back into the cadences of the indigo fields where she had been born." (Indiana)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "folksy" to describe the way Gloria speaks when she is not performing for white audiences—her folksy speech is authentic, a connection to her past.
ex_zh: The novel's folksy moments (Gloria's slips, the slaves' songs, the stories told in the quarters) are all forms of cultural preservation—the folksy is where the resistance lives.

def_en: fond of company; sociable
def_zh: gregarious personality, gregarious and outgoing, gregarious nature
quote_en: The gregarious host made sure every guest felt welcome.
quote_zh: "Sam was gregarious by nature, his cheerfulness a kind of armor against the horrors he witnessed as a station agent on the underground railroad." (Indiana)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "gregarious" to describe Sam's personality, which is a survival mechanism—his gregariousness allows him to make connections, to gather information, to blend in, but it also hides the trauma he carries.
ex_zh: The novel's gregarious characters (Sam, the station agents, the conductors) use sociability as a tool—their gregariousness is not mere friendliness but a strategic adaptation to a dangerous world.

def_en: a person who takes advantage of opportunities without regard for principles
def_zh: political opportunist, opportunist and schemer, rank opportunist
quote_en: The politician was dismissed as an opportunist who would say anything to get elected.
quote_zh: "Mingo was an opportunist who had purchased his family's freedom through careful calculation, but his desire to close the farm to runaways revealed that self-preservation could look very much like betrayal." (Indiana)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "opportunist" to describe Mingo, whose survival strategy is to align himself with power rather than with the fugitives—opportunism is a betrayal of solidarity.
ex_zh: The novel's opportunists (Mingo, the informers, the turncoats) choose self-preservation over community—the novel judges them harshly, but also understands them, because in a slave society, the line between survival and betrayal is not always clear.

def_en: a person holding a high rank or office
def_zh: foreign dignitary, visiting dignitary, dignitary and official
quote_en: Foreign dignitaries gathered at the embassy for the state dinner.
quote_zh: "The dignitaries who attended Big Anthony's roasting—sipping spiced rum while a man burned alive—were not monsters but ordinary men whose humanity had been eroded by the daily practice of slavery." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "dignitary" to describe the powerful men who watch the torture of Big Anthony—their dignity is a mask, and the dignitary is often more monstrous than the lout.
ex_zh: The novel's dignitaries (the judges, the planters, the politicians) are all complicit in the violence of the slave system—their dignity is built on the suffering of the enslaved.

def_en: the act of spreading information widely
def_zh: dissemination of knowledge, dissemination of information, dissemination and distribution
quote_en: The internet has accelerated the dissemination of news across the globe.
quote_zh: "The dissemination of abolitionist literature was illegal in the South, but the underground railroad smuggled pamphlets and newspapers along the same routes that carried fugitives north." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "dissemination" to describe the spread of ideas that threaten the slave system—the dissemination of abolitionist literature is a form of resistance.
ex_zh: The novel's dissemination (of pamphlets, of stories, of the truth about slavery) is the novel's own project—The Underground Railroad is itself an act of dissemination.

def_en: marked with spots or patches of color
def_zh: dappled sunlight, dappled shade, dappled and spotted
quote_en: The dappled sunlight filtered through the leaves of the old oak tree.
quote_zh: "The dappled shadows of the swamp provided cover for the fugitives, but every patch of light might reveal them to the patrollers who hunted them with dogs and torches." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "dappled" to describe the light in the swamp, which is both beautiful and dangerous—the dappled shadows hide the fugitives, but the dappled light can expose them.
ex_zh: The novel's dappled landscapes (the swamp, the forest, the burned fields) are all ambiguous spaces—the dappled light is a metaphor for the fugitive's condition: always between shadow and light.

def_en: to dismiss or make seem unimportant
def_zh: belittle achievements, belittle and demean, belittle the efforts
quote_en: The manager's habit of belittling his employees destroyed team morale.
quote_zh: "The white men of North Carolina sought to belittle the humanity of the colored population, calling them savages and beasts, but the bodies swinging from the Freedom Trail told a different story of who the real savages were." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "belittle" to describe the rhetorical strategy of the slave system—by belittling the humanity of the enslaved, the masters justify their cruelty.
ex_zh: The novel's belittling (of the colored population, of their suffering, of their resistance) is the language of the oppressor—the novel refuses to belittle, insisting instead on the full humanity of the enslaved.

def_en: the student who delivers the farewell speech at graduation
def_zh: class valedictorian, valedictorian speech, valedictorian and salutatorian
quote_en: The valedictorian thanked her parents and teachers in her emotional speech.
quote_zh: "Lander was elected valedictorian of his college after four years of being forced to live in a broom closet, his academic triumph a small revenge against the racism that had tried to define him." (Indiana)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "valedictorian" to describe Lander's achievement, which is a triumph over the racism that tried to confine him—but the novel shows that excellence is not enough to protect you from violence.
ex_zh: The novel's valedictorians (Lander, the educated slaves, the black scholars) achieve excellence despite the system, but the valedictorian who speaks at graduation may be dead before the year is out.

def_en: to promote or make widely known
def_zh: promulgate laws, promulgate a doctrine, promulgate and publicize
quote_en: The organization worked to promulgate the benefits of renewable energy.
quote_zh: "The slave owners promulgated the myth of the happy plantation slave, but the spirituals sung in the quarters told the true story of suffering and the desperate longing for freedom." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "promulgate" to describe the propaganda of the slave system—the masters promulgate a false image of slavery as benevolent, but the spirituals promulgate the truth.
ex_zh: The novel's promulgation (of the slave system's lies, of the fugitives' truths) is the novel's own work—The Underground Railroad promulgates the truth that the slave system tried to hide.

def_en: a sculpted model or representation of a person
def_zh: burn in effigy, effigy of a leader, hang in effigy
quote_en: Protesters burned an effigy of the dictator in the town square.
quote_zh: "Lander's pamphlets were burned in bonfires along with his effigy, but the ideas they contained—of equality, of justice, of a world without slavery—could not be reduced to ash." (Indiana)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "effigy" to describe the symbolic destruction of Lander—the mob burns his effigy, but the effigy is not the man, and the ideas survive the burning.
ex_zh: The novel's effigies (the burned pamphlets, the hanging dummies, the coon show caricatures) are all attempts to control reality through representation—but you cannot burn an idea.

def_en: having irregular patches of two colors, typically black and white
def_zh: piebald horse, piebald pattern, piebald and spotted
quote_en: The piebald horse stood out among the solid-colored herd.
quote_zh: "The piebald horses that pulled the buggy through Indiana were a patchwork of black and white—like the nation itself, a country that claimed liberty while built on the labor of the enslaved." (Indiana)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "piebald" as a metaphor for America—a patchwork of black and white, of liberty and slavery, of freedom and bondage—the piebald nation is a contradiction.
ex_zh: The novel's piebald imagery (the horses, the landscape, the nation) is a visual representation of the central theme—America is piebald, and the attempt to make it all white is a form of violence.

def_en: in a state of severe disrepair
def_zh: ramshackle building, ramshackle cottage, ramshackle and dilapidated
quote_en: The ramshackle cottage looked as if it would collapse in the next storm.
quote_zh: "The ramshackle station beneath the abandoned house had not been used in years, its wooden planks rotting, its tunnel leading nowhere—a ghost of the underground railroad's failed hopes." (Indiana)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "ramshackle" to describe the abandoned stations of the underground railroad—they are ramshackle, falling apart, but they are also sacred spaces, the sites of past resistance.
ex_zh: The novel's ramshackle places (the abandoned stations, the rotting schoolhouse, the burned farm) are all sites of memory—the ramshackle is all that is left, and the ramshackle is enough.

def_en: unpleasantly damp and cold
def_zh: dank cellar, dank and dark, dank and musty
quote_en: The dank basement smelled of mold and decay.
quote_zh: "The dank tunnels of the underground railroad were a second womb for the fugitives—cold, wet, and dark, but the only path to the light of freedom." (Indiana)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "dank" to describe the conditions of the underground railroad—the tunnels are dank, uncomfortable, dangerous, but they are also necessary, and the fugitive must go through the dank to reach the light.
ex_zh: The novel's dank spaces (the tunnels, the cellars, the hiding places) are all places where the fugitive is buried alive—the dank is the condition of the underground, and the underground is the only way north.

def_en: the quality of being certain to happen
def_zh: inevitability of death, inevitability of change, historical inevitability
quote_en: The inevitability of death is a theme explored in many works of literature.
quote_zh: "The inevitability of the Civil War was written in every whipping post and auction block, but even as the nation tore itself apart, the underground railroad kept running, carrying the enslaved toward a future that had to come." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "inevitability" to describe the coming of the Civil War—the war is inevitable because the contradiction between slavery and freedom cannot be sustained forever.
ex_zh: The novel's inevitabilities (the war, the collapse of the slave system, the survival of the fugitives) are both inevitable and contingent—the inevitability does not make the suffering less real.

def_en: a person trained to assist women in childbirth
