def_zh: certified midwife, midwife and doula, midwife's hands
quote_en: The midwife arrived just as the first contractions began.
quote_zh: "Royal's mother was a midwife in Connecticut, bringing new life into the world while her son fought to deliver the enslaved from the death grip of the plantation system." (Indiana)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "midwife" to describe both Royal's mother and Royal himself—she delivers babies, he delivers fugitives, and the work of liberation is a form of midwifery.
ex_zh: The novel's midwives (Royal's mother, the underground railroad agents, the fugitives themselves) are all engaged in the work of delivering the future—liberation is a long, painful labor.

def_en: a person who travels and sells goods
def_zh: street peddler, peddler and hawker, traveling peddler
quote_en: The peddler carried his wares in a large wooden cart pulled by a donkey.
quote_zh: "Valentine's father was a white peddler who passed through every few months, acknowledging his son only within the walls of their house—a reminder that even the love of a father could be stolen by the color line." (Indiana)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "peddler" to describe Valentine's father, a man who moves between worlds but cannot fully belong to either—the peddler is a liminal figure.
ex_zh: The novel's peddlers (Valentine's father, the traveling salesmen, the itinerant workers) are all figures of mobility in a society that wants to keep people in their place.

def_en: a lover, especially one who is not married to their partner
def_zh: secret paramour, paramour and mistress, paramour of the king
quote_en: The queen's paramour was banished from the court after the scandal.
quote_zh: "The slave woman who became her master's paramour bought temporary safety at the cost of her children, who would be sold away as soon as they were old enough to bring a profit." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "paramour" to describe the relationship between master and slave, which is not a love affair but a form of exploitation—the paramour is not a lover but a victim.
ex_zh: The novel's paramours (the enslaved women taken by their masters, the concubines, the victims of sexual violence) are all denied the right to consent—the word "paramour" is a cover for the violence of rape.

def_en: a close friend or companion
def_zh: crony and sidekick, political crony, crony capitalism
quote_en: The mayor appointed his cronies to all the important city positions.
quote_zh: "Mingo and his cronies in the back of the meeting house amened every word he spoke, their loyalty purchased by the promise of a future where they would be the ones deciding who was worthy of freedom." (Indiana)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "crony" to describe Mingo's supporters, who are loyal not to the cause but to their own advancement—the cronies are opportunists.
ex_zh: The novel's cronies (Mingo's allies, the informers, the turncoats) choose power over principle—the novel condemns them, but also understands them, because cronyism is a survival strategy.

def_en: a person who inspires or influences others; a celebrity
def_zh: literary luminary, luminary of the field, luminary and leader
quote_en: The conference attracted luminaries from the world of science and literature.
quote_zh: "The Valentine farm hosted luminaries like Elijah Lander, whose speeches brought tears to the eyes of former slaves who had never heard a black man speak of freedom with such eloquence." (Indiana)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "luminary" to describe Lander, who is a star in the abolitionist world—the luminary is a source of light, but the luminary is also vulnerable, and the light of the luminary attracts bullets.
ex_zh: The novel's luminaries (Lander, the orators, the leaders) are all figures of hope—the novel celebrates them, but it also mourns them, because the luminary is a target.

def_en: not able to be seen through; difficult to understand
def_zh: opaque glass, opaque language, opaque and obscure
quote_en: The professor's explanation was opaque, leaving the students more confused than before.
quote_zh: "Lander was plainspoken but opaque; his speeches moved the heart but left the mind uncertain of what he actually proposed—an ambiguity that made him both inspiring and dangerous." (Indiana)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "opaque" to describe Lander's rhetoric, which is beautiful but unclear—the opacity is a strategy, a way of speaking truth without saying it directly, a way of evading the censor.
ex_zh: The novel's opaque moments (Lander's speeches, the coded messages, the quilts) are all examples of communication that is not transparent—opacity can be a form of protection.

def_en: an essential or urgent thing; a command
def_zh: moral imperative, categorical imperative, imperative to act
quote_en: It is an imperative that we address climate change before it is too late.
quote_zh: "Ridgeway's American imperative—the belief that the white race was destined to conquer and enslave—was nothing more than a justification for theft and murder dressed up as destiny." (Tennessee)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "imperative" to describe Ridgeway's ideology, which he presents as a moral duty but which the novel exposes as a lie—the imperative is a self-serving fiction.
ex_zh: The novel's imperatives (Ridgeway's, the slave system's, the nation's) are all challenged by the novel—the imperative to enslave is a choice, and the imperative to be free is stronger.

def_en: an outline or shape; a line on a map joining points of equal height
def_zh: contour of the land, contour line, contour and silhouette
quote_en: The contour of the mountain was visible against the setting sun.
quote_zh: "The contour of the land told the fugitives where to hide and where to run—the dips and rises of the earth, the curve of the river, the shape of the forest that might shelter them or betray them." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "contour" to describe the landscape that the fugitives must read—the contour is a map, a set of clues, a language that the enslaved must learn.
ex_zh: The novel's contours (the landscape, the tunnels, the bodies) are all surfaces that must be read—the contour of Cora's body is marked by the whip, the contour of the land is marked by the plantation.

def_en: excessively talkative, especially about trivial matters
def_zh: garrulous old man, garrulous and loquacious, garrulous chatter
quote_en: The garrulous old man cornered anyone who would listen to his stories.
quote_zh: "Sam was garrulous by nature, his endless chatter a way of filling the silence that threatened to overwhelm him after the fire that destroyed his house and his friend Caesar." (Indiana)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "garrulous" to describe Sam's talkativeness, which is a defense against silence—the garrulous is not a flaw but a survival strategy, a way of keeping the darkness at bay.
ex_zh: The novel's garrulous characters (Sam, the station agents, the storytellers) are all people who talk to stay alive—their garrulousness is a form of resistance, a refusal to be silent.

def_en: involving immoral or dishonorable actions; dirty
def_zh: sordid affair, sordid details, sordid and squalid
quote_en: The journalist uncovered the sordid details of the corruption scandal.
quote_zh: "The sordid truth of South Carolina's colored uplift program was that the dormitories and schools were a mask for sterilization experiments, turning the dream of freedom into a nightmare of medical exploitation." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "sordid" to describe the reality behind the beautiful surface of South Carolina—the sordid is what is hidden, the dirty secret, the thing that the white people do not want to see.
ex_zh: The novel's sordid revelations (the sterilization program, the medical experiments, the lynchings) are all things that the slave system tries to keep hidden—the novel insists on exposing the sordid truth.

def_en: showing that something is unworthy of consideration
def_zh: dismissive gesture, dismissive tone, dismissive and contemptuous
quote_en: The manager was dismissive of the employees' concerns about safety.
quote_zh: "Ridgeway was dismissive of Terrance Randall's ornate cruelties, but his own methods—the chains, the beatings, the murder of Jasper—were merely a different shade of the same darkness." (Tennessee)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "dismissive" to describe Ridgeway's attitude toward Terrance—he dismisses Terrance's cruelties as excessive, but the novel shows that his dismissiveness is a form of hypocrisy.
ex_zh: The novel's dismissive characters (Ridgeway, the masters, the white townspeople) refuse to see their own complicity—the dismissive is a defense mechanism, a way of maintaining moral superiority while committing atrocities.

def_en: a strong or habitual liking for something
def_zh: penchant for danger, penchant for drama, penchant for violence
quote_en: She had a penchant for collecting antique silver spoons.
quote_zh: "Ridgeway's penchant for violence and odd fixations made it hard to find men willing to ride with him, but his obsession with recapturing Mabel's daughter drove him across state lines." (Tennessee)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "penchant" to describe Ridgeway's character—his penchant for violence is not just a habit but a compulsion, and the novel shows that the penchant is a form of addiction.
ex_zh: The novel's penchants (Ridgeway's for violence, Terrance's for cruelty, the slave system's for exploitation) are all forms of obsession—the penchant that defines a person can also be their undoing.

def_en: an outcast or someone who is avoided
def_zh: social pariah, pariah and outcast, become a pariah
quote_en: After the scandal, he became a pariah in the community he once led.
quote_zh: "Cora was a pariah even among the Hob women, her mother's abandonment marking her as cursed, but it was precisely her outcast status that gave her the courage to run when Caesar came with his plan." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "pariah" to describe Cora's status on the plantation—she is an outcast, avoided by others, but being a pariah is not a weakness but a strength, because the pariah has nothing to lose.
ex_zh: The novel's pariahs (Cora, the Hob women, the fugitives) are all people who have been pushed to the margins—the pariah sees the system from the outside, and the pariah's perspective is the clearest.

def_en: a group of people with a shared characteristic; a companion
def_zh: cohort of students, same cohort, cohort and colleague
quote_en: The cohort of medical students graduated together after four years.
quote_zh: "Ridgeway's cohort of slave catchers had dwindled to Boseman and Homer, the others driven away by his increasing unpredictability and the growing danger of a job that made enemies on both sides of the law." (Tennessee)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "cohort" to describe the group of men who ride with Ridgeway—they are a cohort bound by a shared profession, but the cohort is unstable, and Ridgeway's violence drives them away.
ex_zh: The novel's cohorts (Ridgeway's gang, the patrollers, the night riders) are all groups of men who enforce the slave system—the cohort of the oppressor is as vulnerable as the community of the oppressed.

def_en: showing malicious ill will
def_zh: spiteful remark, spiteful and vindictive, spiteful behavior
quote_en: The spiteful gossip spread rumors about her former friend.
quote_zh: "Ethel was not a cruel woman by nature, but her treatment of Cora—the cold silence, the rare grudging meals—was spiteful in a way that revealed how easily fear could curdle into hatred." (North Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "spiteful" to describe Ethel's treatment of Cora—the spite is not innate but produced by fear, and the novel shows that spite is a response to powerlessness.
ex_zh: The novel's spiteful characters (Ethel, the white townspeople, the informers) are all people who are afraid—spite is the language of fear, and the only cure for spite is courage.

def_en: a descriptive term or phrase; a derogatory word
def_zh: racial epithet, epithet and slur, hurled epithets
quote_en: The racial epithet was scrawled on the wall of the mosque.
quote_zh: "Every slave had heard the epithet 'nigger' countless times—a word designed to strip away humanity, to reduce a person to a thing, to make the whip and the auction block seem natural." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "epithet" to name the most powerful weapon of the slave system—the epithet is not just a word but a tool of dehumanization, a way of making violence acceptable.
ex_zh: The novel's epithets (the racial slurs, the dehumanizing labels) are all present in the novel, but the novel refuses to use them casually—the epithet is a wound, and the novel's job is to heal the wound by telling the truth.

def_en: to instigate or stir up trouble
def_zh: foment rebellion, foment discord, foment and incite
quote_en: The exiled leader was accused of trying to foment revolution from abroad.
quote_zh: "Lander's speeches were said to foment rebellion among the slaves, but the truth was that the rebellion already existed in every heart that beat beneath the lash—he merely gave it words." (Indiana)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "foment" to describe the accusation against Lander—the masters say he foments rebellion, but the novel shows that the rebellion is already there, and the fomenting is not the cause but the expression.
ex_zh: The novel's fomenting (Lander's speeches, the abolitionist pamphlets, the underground railroad) is accused of stirring up trouble, but the novel insists that the trouble is already there—the system is the trouble.

def_en: self-assertive or arrogant, used especially of a person of lower social status
def_zh: uppity servant, uppity and insolent, get uppity
quote_en: The servants were dismissed for being uppity after they requested better working conditions.
quote_zh: "Any slave who looked a white man in the eye, who learned to read, who ran away, was labeled uppity—a word that meant nothing more than refusing to accept the place assigned to you at the bottom of the American order." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "uppity" to name the accusation against any slave who asserts their humanity—the word is a weapon, a way of putting the enslaved in their place, but the novel shows that "uppity" is a compliment.
ex_zh: The novel's uppity characters (Cora, Caesar, the fugitives) are all people who refuse to stay in their place—the novel celebrates their uppityness, shows that uppity is not a flaw but a virtue.

def_en: in a confused, rushed, or disorderly manner
def_zh: run pell-mell, flee pell-mell, pell-mell and helter-skelter
quote_en: The children ran pell-mell down the hill, laughing and shouting.
quote_zh: "After the shooting started, the residents of the Valentine farm fled pell-mell into the darkness, parents losing children, children losing parents, each fugitive suddenly alone again." (Indiana)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "pell-mell" to describe the chaos of the massacre—the flight is pell-mell, disorderly, desperate, and the novel shows that the pell-mell is the reality of violence.
ex_zh: The novel's pell-mell moments (the massacre, the escapes, the panics) are all scenes of chaos—the novel refuses to impose order on them, refuses to pretend that there is a pattern, and the pell-mell is the texture of the fugitive's experience.

def_en: a heavy iron block on which metal is hammered
def_zh: hammer and anvil, anvil and forge, blacksmith's anvil
quote_en: The blacksmith's hammer rang against the anvil from dawn until dusk.
quote_zh: "Ridgeway's father had worked the anvil, shaping iron into chains and plows, but his son shaped human beings into fugitives and corpses—two sides of the same American imperative, hammer and anvil, master and slave." (South Carolina)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "anvil" as a metaphor for the slave system—the anvil is the hard surface on which things are forged, and the novel shows that America was forged on the anvil of slavery.
ex_zh: The novel's anvil (the forge, the hammer, the chain) is the central image of the American project—the anvil is where the nation was made, and the anvil is also where the nation can be unmade, where new freedoms can be forged.

def_en: a soft, moist mass applied to the body to relieve soreness or inflammation
def_zh: apply a poultice, poultice of herbs, poultice and bandage
quote_en: The herbalist prepared a poultice of crushed leaves to treat the wound.
quote_zh: "The Hob women applied a poultice of roots and brine to Cora's whip-scarred back, their gentle hands a small mercy in a world that offered the enslaved nothing but suffering." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "poultice" to describe the care that the enslaved give to each other—the poultice is a small act of kindness, a moment of healing in a world of violence, and a form of resistance.
ex_zh: The novel's poultices (the Hob women's remedies, the care of the community, the small acts of kindness) are all moments of grace in a brutal world—the poultice is a sign of solidarity, a refusal to let the system win.

def_en: to blow away chaff from grain; to reduce a large group
def_zh: winnow the wheat, winnow out the weak, winnow and sift
quote_en: The coach had to winnow the fifty applicants down to a team of twelve.
quote_zh: "The early death of most slave children was a brutal winnowing, leaving only the strongest to survive the fields—a calculation as cold as the one that separated wheat from chaff." (Georgia)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "winnow" to describe the process by which the slave system selects for survival—the winnowing is brutal, efficient, and engineered, not natural, a form of eugenics.
ex_zh: The novel's winnowing (the deaths of children, the selection of the strong, the discard of the weak) is the hidden logic of the plantation—the novel exposes the winnowing, shows that it is not an accident but a design, and that the winnowed are the ones who run.

def_en: bad-tempered or combative; stubborn
def_zh: ornery old man, ornery and cantankerous, ornery disposition
quote_en: The ornery old mule refused to move no matter how much the farmer tugged.
quote_zh: "The snake was ornery but harmless, or so Mabel told herself before the cottonmouth's fangs sank into her thigh—a reminder that even the most ordinary creature could carry death in its bite." (Mabel)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "ornery" to describe the snake that kills Mabel—the snake is ornery, stubborn, but the novel shows that the ornery can kill you, that the ornery is the unexpected danger.
ex_zh: The novel's ornery characters (the snake, the patrollers, the small cruelties) are all things that seem minor but are deadly—the ornery is the texture of the slave system, the small, daily, stubborn cruelties that wear you down.

def_en: a disorderly retreat; to defeat decisively
def_zh: put to rout, complete rout, rout the enemy
quote_en: The army's rout began when the general fled the battlefield.
quote_zh: "The white posse's rout of the Valentine farm left bodies scattered across the meeting house floor, but the survivors who fled into the night would carry the memory of that massacre like a torch—one day to be lit again." (Indiana)
ex_en: Whitehead uses "rout" to describe the destruction of the Valentine farm—the rout is a defeat, a massacre, but the novel shows that the rout is not the end, that the survivors carry the memory, and that the routed are not defeated because they survive.
ex_zh: The novel's routs (the Valentine farm, the underground railroad stations, the hopes of the fugitives) are all moments of destruction—but the novel also shows that the rout is not the final chapter, that the routed survivors will rebuild, and that the idea of freedom will outlast any rout.
